100% agree with @vsboost. YS1 is not a SDR. It is just an "advanced breakout board" for the [TI CC1111](http://www.ti.com/lit/gpn/cc1110-cc1111) platform. It is by far not as flexible as a SDR. You have a limited set of input parameters you can define (like preamble, syncword, modulation, etc.) and it will decode the data for you.

I don't think there is a good way to integrate this into the workflow of URH. Well, at least the whole interpretation phase is pointless with the YS1. Only if you already know the parameters of the protocol you investigating, you can decode it with the YS1. However, using the fuzzer of URH for a YS1 might be handy. But on the other side, it is easy to implement a small fuzzer for YS1 using rfcat...

The manual page can be generated by running `make -C docs man` in the yq repo directory.

I'm still not convinced that this set_wakeup_fd() is a practical concern in practice. If Python gets a signal, it writes to that file.  If there's a chance it might overflow buffer space because nothing is listening, then make the buffer bigger. Or, don't write code that blocks the signal listener for long periods.  

Regarding earlier messages----is there some particular reason someone would be fooling around with SIGCHLD in combination with the subprocess module?   Functions in that module already reap the exit code.  There's almost no reason to be writing a SIGCHLD handler to avoid zombies there.  Maybe I'm missing something obvious.

Every timeout in Curio is initiated by the timeout_after() function.  I'm proposing that any TaskTimeout exception that propagates out of it have an expired flag to indicate whether or not the timeout period given to timeout_after() has expired or not.  

One of my general concerns is that timeout handling is already quite tricky.   I'm somewhat hesitant to make it even more tricky.  However, putting a simple boolean flag on the exception to indicate whether or not the specified time interval has expired seems like it would be rather straightforward.  It would also solve the initial problem of knowing if an inner timeout has expired or not.

The value of the expired flag on any timeout would depend entirely on the enclosing `timeout_after()`  operation---essentially any TaskTimeout exception that propagated out of that (either used as a function or as a context manager) would have the flag set according to whatever time internal was specified.

This cdna branch is concluded: it has been merged into master. The next release of minimap2 will support spliced alignment. As of now, minimap2 still doesn't find precise exon boundaries, but if you allow ~10bp differences, it is *slightly* more sensitive and specific than gmap on 2D nanopore reads from [Byrne et al (2017)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28722025), and is ~30X faster against the mouse genome. Probably gmap can be tuned for improved accuracy. I haven't tried as I am not familiar with its internal. I will play with how to find precise boundaries, but the feature won't come soon.

The following screenshot shows minimap2 (track r289.bam) and gmap alignment, as well as the truth isoforms. They are very close.

![screen shot 2017-08-12 at 13 20 13](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/480346/29244114-4188fc0c-7f7e-11e7-9d04-a8d080d4ffa4.png)

The genome data is still private, but it wouldn't be a problem to share a few reads that cause the issue with you if that helps to get to the bottom of it.

I ran two tests following your suggestion, one with the -a tag to produce SAM output, and one without, just to see if this mattered. The commands are below:

```
minimap2 -x ava-pb -t 1 --print-qname -a $FILEDIR/$FILENAME $FILEDIR/$FILENAME > /dev/null 2> run1.log
minimap2 -x ava-pb -t 1 --print-qname $FILEDIR/$FILENAME $FILEDIR/$FILENAME > /dev/null 2> run2.log
```

This seems to have made a difference, since the run with the -a tag failed with the assertion error, whereas the run without the -a tag has made it past this point and is still running as of now. The run that failed had this as the last two lines:

```
QR      m000_000/16037/0_17464  0
minimap2: align.c:470: mm_align1: Assertion `qs1 >= 0 && rs1 >= 0' failed.
```

Do you want me to send this m000_000/16037/0_17464 read (or the one immediately after it)? Let me know anything else you'd like me to try as well.

Hi @caduri, 

I've added a prototype implementation of slice based pagination. The current implementation has some limitations:

* Does not support indexing at all
* Does not support setting `per_page`
* Does not support negative values
* Always pulls the first 100 objects
* Does not support multiple accesses

On the bright side, it has a few advantages:

* Supports simple use cases, like retrieving ticket 720 to 750  efficiently (two API calls, could be optimized to one)
* The implementation is isolated from the rest of the Zenpy code, which should make it easier for people to hack on without worrying about breaking things
* All of the above limitations could theoretically be overcome, making it really awesome and powerful

Example usage:
```python
ticket_generator = zenpy_client.tickets()
tickets = ticket_generator[720:750]
print(tickets)
```

You can try it out here - https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/tree/support_pagination.

You can do this as follow:

```python

```

Hi, there,

thanks for the note and sorry about the confusion regarding the the`refit` parameter ... what I had in mind was that say s.o. already fit the classifiers that go into `self.clfs_` and doesn't want to refit them -- e.g., because it is computationally too expensive.

(I think how you interpreted it would offer a replacement for sth. like pickle?)

Now, when you do sth like

```
from sklearn import datasets

iris = datasets.load_iris()
X, y = iris.data[:, 1:3], iris.target

from sklearn import model_selection
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB 
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from mlxtend.classifier import EnsembleVoteClassifier
import numpy as np

clf1 = LogisticRegression(random_state=1)
clf2 = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1)
clf3 = GaussianNB()

clf1.fit(X, y)
clf2.fit(X, y)
clf3.fit(X, y)

eclf = EnsembleVoteClassifier(clfs=[clf1, clf2, clf3], weights=[1,1,1], refit=False)
eclf.fit(X, y)
```

the `EnsembleVoteClassifier` won't create empty clones of `clf1`, `clf2`, `clf3` and refit them to `X y`, since that may be wasteful if that's been done before. One scenario where this would be particularly wasteful would be hyperparameter tuning of the `weights` since the `clf1`, `clf2`, `clf3` would be refit to the same training set every time if `refit=True` -- by setting `refit=False`, one can play around with the `weights` without refitting.

Hope that makes sense, and I'd be very happy about a PR to clarify the meaning of the `refit` param!

PS: For what you had in mind, there would be a somewhat "hacky" workaround, assigning classifiers to  `clfs` "manually"

```
eclf = EnsembleVoteClassifier(clfs=['void'], weights=[1,1,1], refit=False)

clf1.fit(X, y)
clf2.fit(X, y)
clf3.fit(X, y)

eclf.clfs = [clf1, clf2, clf3]

eclf.fit(X, y)
```

@stef Well, consider this example:

This is my experience so far with `pysodium` when developing a multi-platform `asyncio`-native implementation of ZeroMQ.

    pip install pysodium

I then tried to used only to discover I had to install `libsodium` externally.

On Linux, I basically had to `apt-get install libsodium`. On OSX, I had to `brew install libsodium`. So far, easy enough.

On Windows, I had to compile my own `libsodium.dll` and put it in some folder. I then had to add that folder to the `PATH`. Surely, the lack of standard and package manager on Windows is to blame, but from a user perspective, using `pysodium` could (should ?) be OS agnostic. I should just have to do the `pip install` and the fact it uses a C library underneath should be an implementation detail.

For instance, `pyzmq` does exactly what I suggest, and embeds the precompiled binaries for Windows, OSX and compiles it on Linux on package install. As a result, I can do `pip install zmq` and use the package. The fact it relies on a C library is hidden to the user.

Now to answer to your pros/cons list:

Pros
----

- Better user experience.
- OS agnosticity (that may not be a word. Forgive my french).
- No incompatibility with the installed library: we always use one we control.
- Related to the previous point: ability to use different versions in different virtual environments without messing with `PATH`/`LD_LIBRARY_PATH`.

Cons
----
- No easy way for the user to control which version of `libsodium` to use. If a new `libsodium` is released, and we don't release a new `pysodium`, the user has to wait or fork/patch.
- A slightly more complex `setup.py`: `cffi` makes this easy enough, but there is still some work to do. Maintenance should be low though.

@davisking I was expecting this (and I'm totally on your side). Using a C++ library (even of such stellar quality as dlib truly is) requires intricate knowledge of build systems, flags, preprocessor macros, shared-vs-static libraries and what not. Yes, I feel your pain, and totally sympathize with it. This is why I initially suggested using static libraries, because for beginner-user-joe-sixpack, `-ldlib` works (given that you're also providing the private linking flags). Shared libraries are oftentimes more involved, and require `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`, which is generally beyond the reach of normal users.

I think it goes without saying that any breakage caused by this has to be picked up by me, given that I am the one trying to get this into dlib.

I have a different suggestion for you if you consider my proposal too invasive (and it's not without risk, given that anyone one configuration will never again produce both static and shared libraries simultaneously):

Add an explicit (non-CMake idiomatic) `BUILD_STATIC_LIBS` flag that is `ON` by default. This will lead to a situation where by default nothing really changes, both static and shared libs are produced. Yet, it allows us to disable building of static libs. This is not my favoured approach, because it goes against best practices and NIHes new variables, but it wouldn't change anything by default for users, unless they start mucking with those flags.

Which approach do you prefer?

from jpype import *

startJVM(getDefaultJVMPath())

在Windows下就报UnicodeEncodeError错了，说明问题与pyhanlp无关。
jpype在进行str到String的转换过程中就发生了错误，那么解决方案就简单了。转换回来的时候忽略错误即可：

```
from jpype import *

startJVM(getDefaultJVMPath())
text = u'无法处理的情况\U0001F923'
print(text)
print(str(java.lang.String(text)).encode('utf-16', 'ignore').decode('utf-16'))
```

对你的程序来讲，可以这么做：
```
from pyhanlp import *

text = u'无法处理的情况\U0001F923'
for term in HanLP.segment(text):
    print(term.word.encode('utf-8', 'ignore').decode('utf-8'))
```

Jason,
I appreciate your interest in fixing keyring library issues. I'm sure it's not your fault that it took so long - different people were handling issues at the time.

Time has passed and the source code of keyring changed, so some of my concerns are not applicable anymore right now.

Nevertheless, I think that the time I took writing the patch and the time I waited for a response would deserve a bit more thorough review of my patch. You didn't need to apply it - it would make little sense on today's codebase, I suppose - but an answer like "I'm sorry we took so much time. Your patch is now outdated, and we won't accept it" would have been much, much better.

1) the patch is stale -> of course I can do nothing about that

2) the patch attempts to doo to much -> maybe a really small bit. Maybe it would have been good to split the _keyring_backend modification and the better import. But at the time the project was quite messy and the small modification of adding a _keyring_backend (which was a good idea, now a similar result is achieved in init_backend(), I see) at the beginning in a single commit would do little harm. The other part is quite a lot of code, but from a semantic POV it's a single change.

3) Sure, it may be deprecated. By the way python 2.7 with importlib was just a few months old when I released this patch, and at the time I thought that it was a bit limiting to prevent python < 2.7 to work with keyring, or to require an external library for a single functionality. In hindsight, today importlib would be a good choice.

4) There's no comment but the code is clear. The original code made little sense. There's no way ConfigParser could raise the error - the original code checks for such an option in a safe way ( config.get ... ) then raises NoOptionError and catches it later. Since the patch proposal makes the code much shorter, such internal try/catching makes no sense and it's just a matter of if/else. Behaviour is just the same.

5) That's true; I should probably have deleted that as well, since my idea was to just let the keyring be loaded from its FQDN, so it should be in sys.path elsewere. I still think that's a better way to go (manual fiddling with load path is a bad idea, IMHO)

6) "NoneType" has no attribute "something" tells much more than a stray NameError. I couldn't fix everything with a single patch. A more complex proposal I submitted years ago to the keyring ML (no answer, of course) said that the current keyring implementation sucks, and we should create a keyring object with different implementations which could then be used by client programs, instead of relying on free functions that rely on global configuration.

I took my time to discuss keyring design and I had little response:

https://groups.google.com/forum/?fromgroups#!topic/python-keyring/EFl45ddWEPM

https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/wiki/Redesign

(yes, I wrote that last page, if anyone noticed it)

Hence I think I don't deserve such treatment.

Just in case, decided to measure performance of `partition` vs `re`, here is the code that was used:

```python
import re

ESCAPE_DECODE_RE = re.compile(r'\\(.)|\\()$', flags=(re.MULTILINE | re.DOTALL))
ESCAPE_CHARS = {
    't': '\t',
    'n': '\n',
    'r': '\r',
    '\\': '\\',
}


def parse_field_partition(s):
    o = ''
    if s == '\\N':
        return None
    before, sep, after = s.partition('\\')
    while sep != '':
        o += before
        if after == '':
            raise ValueError('Final backslash is forbidden.')
        if after[0] in ESCAPE_CHARS:
            o += ESCAPE_CHARS[after[0]]
            before, sep, after = after[1:].partition('\\')
        else:
            before, sep, after = after.partition('\\')
    else:
        o += before
        return o


def _escape_decode(match):
    char = match.group(1)
    return ESCAPE_CHARS.get(char, char or '')


def parse_field_re(s):
    if s == '\\N':
        return None
    return ESCAPE_DECODE_RE.sub(_escape_decode, s)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    from timeit import timeit

    def run(s):
        ns = dict(globals(), s=s)
        a = timeit("parse_field_partition(s)", globals=ns)
        b = timeit("parse_field_re(s)", globals=ns)
        p = 100 / max(a, b) * (max(a, b) - min(a, b))
        s = '%s...(+%d chars)' % (s[:16], len(s) - 16) if len(s) > 24 else s
        print('%9.2f | %5.2f | %7.0f%% | %r' % (a, b, p, s))

    print('partition | re    | % faster | string')
    print('----------+-------+----------+---------')

    run('1\t2')
    run('\t'.join(map(str, range(100))))
    run('\t'.join(map(str, range(1000))))
```

And these are results:

```
partition | re    | % faster | string
----------+-------+----------+---------
     0.43 |  0.74 |      41% | '1\t2'
     0.45 |  0.95 |      53% | '0\t1\t2\t3\t4\t5\t6\t7\t...(+273 chars)'
     0.56 |  3.44 |      84% | '0\t1\t2\t3\t4\t5\t6\t7\t...(+3873 chars)'
```

`partition` is at least two times faster than `re`.

These are good ones! Nice. A bit of feedback:

* I think the extra value checks in `strpdate` are kind of extraneous. If someone wants to parse a date out from a string with hhmmss in it, let them, I say. Of course removing that turns it into a trivial function. But still one I feel like would have made for improved parallelism for the `datetime.date` type. So still good and mergeable
* `daterange` is also good. That said, I see some issues. 
    * The first is that `assert`s are kind of bad form in this case. They can be disabled and don't provide great error messages. Explicit checks with good `ValueError`s and `TypeError`s are preferable.
    * The second is that day-only ranges are tough for iterating through months and years. Leap years throw it off. And months are of course all different lengths. So we could either complicate this function a lot, or keep it simple. A name change. How about, `dayrange`?

weitere Events findet man in der Status-Ausgabe (Web oder Konsole) unter events.
Ein Event kann in die Konfig-Datei integriert werden in dem es als einzelne Sektion geschrieben wird. Es muss dabei EVENT_ vorangestellt werden um eine Abgrenzung zu anderen Konfig-Einträgen deutlich zu machen.
z.B. 
    [EVENT_OnTimeMinuteEvery5]
    10 = statusfile:/var/log/doorpi/status.log,last status: %Y-%m-%d %H-%M-%S

Durch die 10 als Key in dieser Sektion wird die Reihenfolge der Aktionen definiert, sofern mehrere vorhanden sind.
    [EVENT_OnTimeMinuteEvery5]
    10 = statusfile:/var/log/doorpi/status.log,last status: %Y-%m-%d %H-%M-%S
    20 = statusfile:/home/pi/tmp/hallomeldung.log,ich bin die zweite Datei

in dem Fall würde zuerst die Status-Datei unter /var/log/doorpi/status.log geschrieben und erst danach die Datei /home/pi/tmp/hallomeldung.log

> This is done by extending the API to support either implicit timing (start times only, the old behavior) or interval timing (start, end times), and modifying the synthesis accordingly.
> This change should be backward compatible.

Looks reasonable to me.  Does it do what you want it to do now?

>  I think fast_synthesize could be made more efficient using stride tricks to simulate a much larger buffer

Go for it, if you want!

> For "low" sampling rates (ie 22050) there's some audible modulation due to the not-quite-repeating synthesis buffer
> We could avoid transient pops by aligning start/end times to the closest zero crossing in the buffer. I really hate these pops :(

Neither of these bother me - this is not supposed to be a high-quality resynthesis, nor is it really possible to create one out of a piano roll, arguably.  Also, using `scipy.signal.square` as the periodic function will suppress both these issues, and I like using square waves any ways because they sound a little nicer when you're resynthesizing time-frequency representations, but that's beside the point.  If you want to address either/both, feel free, but finding the nearest zero crossing sounds like it will get a little ugly.  A nice solution to the pops would be to smoothly interpolate between amplitude levels, which would be extremely straightforward with the original start-time-only call structure but less than straightforward with non-overlapping intervals.

On Windows, even at C level, only a handful of signal constants are available: `SIGABRT`, `SIGFPE`, `SIGILL`, `SIGINT`, `SIGSEGV`, and `SIGTERM`; `SIGKILL` is not among them. (https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/c-runtime-library/reference/signal#remarks)

Thus, the `signal` module on Windows does not contain `SIGKILL` either, only `SIGTERM`.

```
>py -3
Python 3.6.3 (v3.6.3:2c5fed8, Oct  3 2017, 18:11:49) [MSC v.1900 64 bit (AMD64)] on win32
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import signal
>>> signal.SIGTERM
<Signals.SIGTERM: 15>
>>> signal.SIGKILL
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: module 'signal' has no attribute 'SIGKILL'
```

And the doc of `subprocess.Popen.kill` also declares that "[o]n Windows kill() is an alias for terminate()". (https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html#subprocess.Popen.kill)

from ibapi.wrapper import EWrapper
from ib_insync import *

from threading import Thread
import asyncio

class TestClient(Client, EWrapper):

    def __init__(self):
        EWrapper.__init__(self)
        Client.__init__(self, self)

    def tickPrice(self, *args):
        print('tickPrice', *args)

    def tickSize(self, *args):
        print('tickSize', *args)

    def start(self):
        self.thread = Thread(target=self.run)
        self.thread.start()

    def run(self):
        loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
        asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)

        loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        loop.run_forever()

Hm.

The last fix change it so that we request scopes and get MORE than we need, we don't complain. This, on the other hand, would be an actual problem: If we request push, but don't get push, that _is_ an error and potential problem.

I think the correct thing here to do for brutaldon would be to in fact not request push scopes - essentially, what's going on currently is that (because the default is to request read, write and push), and since mastodon.py has no way to know that you weren't going to use those push scopes, it lets you know "hey! something went wrong! the server doesn't want to let you do this thing you are trying to do!". 

For mastodon.py, I wonder if we should just not request push scopes by default - I doubt many people are actually using them currently anyways.

After some consideration, I realized that having the hooks in the Graph class itself just adds more complexity to the Graph implementation. And, since any implementation is going to need to create a custom subclass to implement the hooks, it's a small step to include a mix-in. Now, svg.charts.graph provides a mix-in class which can be readily used for this purpose.

Plus, since the functionality is in a separate class, it's very easy to see which hooks are defined, that their default implementation is empty, and how to define them. This feature will be in the 3.3 release shortly.

Don't use DataTables *and* tablesorter together.

I started working on a demo for you this morning, then I found some issues:

* In a table with the pager applied to multiple tbodies:
  * How do you count total rows? I think counting by page would be the solution, but just in case the output were to include a row count, it wouldn't make sense, or would only make sense for the last tbody.
  * How do you count filtered rows?
  * What if each tbody has a different number of rows, what do you do when the page is beyond that tbody? Show nothing? Stay on the last page? Everything would get unsynchronized if you use that approach.
  * How would you differentiate each row count? Would each tbody need a spacer with a custom output?

Then I thought, maybe I could just add multiple tables, all controlled by one pager:

* First off, the pager is not set up to allow multiple bindings to the same set of controls. So, I added event namespace bindings to fix that.
* The same problem with counting rows shows up again.
* Again, what if each table had a different number of rows? Would you show an empty tbody? If the [`fixedHeight` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#pager-fixedheight) were set, a nice big empty gap would show up in that empty table.

So, I'm starting to think that maybe the best solution would be to have multiple tables, each with their own pager.

I am open to feedback on how to implement your idea. What are you expecting as a result?

Yes, I think we came to the conclusion at the same time that the multiple thead structure is not really valid.  But to further the discussion, the sort was applied because the rows in the tbody sections would sort when clicking the header.  We will move to a one thead/multiple tbody structure, changing those lower thead to tbody with the no sort class.  This should fix the problem, but the one thing it removes is the ability to click on the secondary thead headers to sort the table.

Basically the structure was NFL divisional standings, so multiple thead/tbody in one table for each division.  Clicking on any of the headers was actually sorting all the tbody sections, which we wanted, but the sort class was not being applied.  I imagine it had to do with it processing the "other" thead headers and messing up the logic in determining whether or not a column was sorted.

Updated demo here [here](http://jsfiddle.net/856bzzeL/777/)  As you can see, the top headers do not change sort class, but interestingly the second thead does.

```python
data = [ (1), (2) ]
```

is not a list of tuples, it is a list of individual numeric values. That statement is actually the same as

```python
data = [ 1, 2 ]
```

If you want a list of tuples you need to use

```python
data = [ (1,), (2,) ]
```

I'm fine with this change, but I don't think it's actually going to make much difference unfortunately. In testing, there's ~100 bytes size difference between the version with the class attributes of `param_defaults` and version with the instance attributes. Using the following program to test each version:

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import pickle
import sys
import twitter

CONSUMER_KEY = 'WWW'
CONSUMER_SECRET = 'XXX'
ACCESS_TOKEN_KEY = 'YYY'
ACCESS_TOKEN_SECRET = 'ZZZ'

if __name__ == '__main__':
    api = twitter.Api(CONSUMER_KEY, CONSUMER_SECRET, ACCESS_TOKEN_KEY, ACCESS_TOKEN_SECRET)
    tweets = api.GetUserTimeline(count=1000)  # about 89 tweets for my test account
    print(sum([sys.getsizeof(s) for s in tweets]))
    tweets_pickled = [pickle.dumps(s) for s in tweets]
    print(sum([len(s) for s in tweets_pickled]))
```

Class attribute version
```python
4984  # sys.getsizeof
438950  # len of pickled (give or take, varies with each run)
```

Instance attribute version:
```python
4984  # sys.getsizeof
448183  # len of pickled (give or take, varies with each run)
```

In terms of performance, looking at this [article from 2013](https://www.toptal.com/python/python-class-attributes-an-overly-thorough-guide) (on python 2.7.2 admittedly), seems like it's kind of a wash performance-wise. In my testing, the difference is negligible with class variables being very slightly faster for initialization:

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import timeit

if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Instance attribute version
    t = timeit.timeit(
        'twitter.models.Status.NewFromJsonDict(STATUS_SAMPLE_JSON)',
        setup="""import json; import twitter; f = open('testdata/models/models_status.json', 'rb'); STATUS_SAMPLE_JSON = json.loads(f.read().decode('utf8'))""")
    print("Instance attribute initialization: ", t)

    # Class attribute version
    t2 = timeit.timeit(
        'twitter.models.StatusTwo.NewFromJsonDict(STATUS_SAMPLE_JSON)',
        setup="""import json; import twitter; f = open('testdata/models/models_status.json', 'rb'); STATUS_SAMPLE_JSON = json.loads(f.read().decode('utf8'))""")
    print("Class attribute initialization: ", t2)
```

Output:
```python
Instance attribute initialization:  47.60752862901427
Class attribute initialization:  46.76807844999712
```

(The `testdata/models/models_status.json` file referenced is the same one as in the Github repo, so you can run these tests yourself.)

These tests were run only changing the `twitter.models.Status` object, so changing all the twitter models may yield larger gains (since `Status` objects contain `User`, `Url`, `Media`, etc. objects)

I was going to file a bug but I'll attach it to this bug. In TimeSignature, you don't keep around the notated 32nd-notes in a MIDI quarter-note (24 MIDI Clocks) so when you write a file, this information is lost. An incoming TimeSignature might be:  
midi.TimeSignatureEvent(tick=0, data=[6, 3, 36, 8]). 
If you load that into pretty_midi and write it back out you get:
midi.TimeSignatureEvent(tick=0, data=[6, 3, 0, 0]),

If you don't want to store info, you could at least set some reasonable default such as you do when you write the default 4/4 time signature:
   timing_track += [midi.TimeSignatureEvent(tick=0, data=[4, 2, 24, 8])]
(24 midi clocks per metronome click, 8 32nd notes in a midi qn)

That seems like a reasonable default. I personally don't care all that much. I just don't want these files to break some sequencer software because of 0 values in these fields.

@deanmcguire random question for you here... I just realized that the library is leaking Ping() reply messages, since Ping() calls Notify() and not NotifyAndGetResponse().

Since you're probably the heaviest user of the HandleEvents() method, I wanted to get your input about this issue.

There are a couple of ways to handle things, but I'm on the fence. Calling NotifyAndGetResponse() inside of Ping() feels like the right thing todo since it's really sending a message to the event stream and a reply is sent in response.
The problem is that if you call HandleEvents(), like your code does, then HandleEvents can possibly "steal" the response for Ping(), which would cause NotifyAndGetResponse() to eventually timeout.
One possible mitigation for this would be to have HandleEvents() check the message for "subscriptions" in the resource element of the event message. If it starts with "subscriptions", then I could just re-queue that message in hopes that Ping() eventually gets it. 
Something like this:
```
            while basestation_id in self.event_streams and self.event_streams[basestation_id].connected:
                event = self.event_streams[basestation_id].Get(block=True, timeout=timeout)
                if event:
                    if event.get('resource').startswith('subscriptions'):
                        self.event_streams[basestation_id].queue.put(event)
                    else:
                        response = callback(self, basestation, event)
                        # NOTE: Not ideal, but this allows you to look for a specific event and break if you want to return it.
                        if response is not None:
                            return response
```
There are a couple of issues with this approach.
1) The event for Ping() might keep getting handled by HandleEvents() when it's re-queued, which could impact performance, and possibly lead to an infinite loop.
2) Someone might want to do something with the "subscriptions", but it would be put back into the queue before we allow someone to handle it.

So, my big question to you is... Do you foresee any need to handle Ping() reply events in your HandleEvents() callback?

All `data` feeds have basic parameters. One of them is the `dataname` which is the actual symbol. This use was not yet taken into account ... and hence the fact that this parameter in the case of *resampling/replaying* is no longer the symbol but actually the `data` which is being *resampled/replayed*.

This really deserves a small patch. Meanwhile you could do something like

```python
dataname = data.p.dataname  # or the full version data.params.dataname
if not isinstance(dataname, backtrader.utils.py3.string_types):
    dataname = dataname.p.dataname
```

This undoes the implied recursion of *resampling/replying*

There's a pattern emerging here.  

1. People can't read the SPEC [documentation](https://certif.com/spec_manual/mac_3_13.html).
2. The parser must be more robust for lines such as:

```
#T 0.5  (Seconds)
#M 200000 (I0)
```

The string provided in parentheses is not necessary since the standard macros put the column with the counting reference (time or monitor) ALWAYS in the next to last column.

Nevertheless, the parser should be changed to strip the #T or #M away and then parse only the next number.  Anything else on the line should be ignored since it has no value.

This is more a pythonic issue: The Python interpreter does not automatically apply the environment locale to it's internal localisation machinery.

Use the following code to get this fixed:

```python
import locale
import rrdtool

locale.setlocale(locale.LC_ALL, 'de_DE')
rrdtool.graph(…)
```

If you depend on using the locale as set by environment variables, you may use the following:

```python
import locale
import rrdtool

locale.setlocale(locale.LC_ALL, '')  # this will use the locale as set in the environment variables
rrdtool.graph(…)

@carlescufi 
There's subtle differences in behavior I think, e.g. when the file exists in the working directory but not in `$srctree`. I think that version would also add `$srctree` as a prefix to `MenuNode.filename`, which is inconsistent.

Here's what I had in mind:

When `$srctree` is set, `source` searches the working directory before `$srctree` (I suspect no one ever uses that, but it's what the C tools do, and it was easy to implement). Defining what the equivalent behavior for a globbing source should be gets tricky, so I decided to keep it simple and restrict `gsource` to only search `$srctree` when set.

Something like

    gsource "foo*"

would then search `$srctree` and turn into the equivalent of e.g.

    source "foo1"
    source "foo2"
    source "foo3"

You could then still have `foo1`, `foo2`, etc. files in the current directory if you wanted to "override" those files. The only thing you'd lose would be the ability to have globbed files exist in the working directory but not in `$srctree`. I suspect no one would need that, and the only-search-`$srctree` (but allow overriding) behavior is simpler to understand and gives simpler code.

It's splitting hairs anyway, as I might be the only one who cares about those corner cases. :P

Oups, I just realized you fixed this a while ago!

It seems the error was caused by the `[check]` section in your `setup.cfg` which caused setuptools to always try and do the check on install, which required `docutils` and `Pygments` to be there to install the package. This isn't good for cross compatibility. It fails on Travis because the Travis environment has very little installed by default, and adding more packages to fix this probably isn't a good idea.

Instead of this I've separated the `check` command to be run under `tox` in a new environment (it doesn't work on Python 2.6) (P.S. I'd consider dropping Python 2.6 support atm since it's getting very old).

In my projects I normally have a separate `codestyle` tox environment ([example](https://github.com/adamchainz/django-mysql/blob/master/tox.ini#L19)) that I point to run a number of checks like `flake8` ([example](https://github.com/adamchainz/django-mysql/blob/master/runtests.py#L32)).

Also I've tidied up your `setup.py` a little in the process of debugging this. First we no longer import the package to get `__version__` - this is important as in some environments it won't be possible to import the file. And second we no longer leave a file handle to `README.rst` open, by using the `with` form of `open`.

Hi Jeff. Yeah, there is a lot of changes going on at the moment so maybe it's better to wait some time for it to settle down.

> Yes, actions with side effects currently don't work with GLR, so things are no worse off than they are now, right?

I would like for LR and GLR to stay as compatible as possible so user could start with LR because of the parser speed and deterministic behavior. If she sees that she needs more look-ahead for a parser to decide what to do or that language is truly ambiguous then she could just switch to GLR without changes.

>  However, wouldn't the context cloning changes proposed in #50 allow the side effects to work with GLR?

Yes, it looks like that could work. However, we should be cautious not to introduce performance degradation. parglare is used in production and there are projects that could benefit of improvements in performance, so at least we should strive to stay where we are now with each new feature. My gut tells me that this shouldn't degrade performance significantly but we can't be sure before it's done and tested. There are some performance tests in `tests/perf` folder. They are by no means proper but I find them useful to see how good or bad some change affects performance.

Your implementation assumes that we're always using a function that always returns something wrapped in the identity object, which means that it doesn't support `get` or `get_all`. Supporting them would be a requirement for merging.

There's not any principled way to implement this, as far as I know, because that would require you to be able to join the multiple result objects together (i.e. that they are monads, which const is certainly not). You could maybe implement this by peeking inside the object and messing around with its internals. Very dirty, but I would not be wholly against that.

Please ignore my previous message. The FB API changes caused my own code to break, not python-social-auth.

Here is my solution to solve this problem

> django + python-social-auth

1.  modify `MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES`
MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES = (
  ...
  'social.apps.django_app.middleware.SocialAuthExceptionMiddleware',
)

2. setup login error url
ex: SOCIAL_AUTH_LOGIN_ERROR_URL = '/accounts/login-error/'

3. setup login error view what you want
4. as [Exceptions Middleware](https://goo.gl/cgBvqk) mention. Exception processing is disabled if any of this settings is defined with a True value:

```
<backend name>_SOCIAL_AUTH_RAISE_EXCEPTIONS = True
SOCIAL_AUTH_RAISE_EXCEPTIONS = True
RAISE_EXCEPTIONS = True
DEBUG = True
```

- Fetch the calendar item **from R1 mailbox**.
- Attempt to save it.

So this happens when you attempt to edit a calendar item not by impersonating the organizer but the attendee/resource instead.

The related fields on Django models perform database lookups, so unless you save the revision to the database, they will always reference the live data.

There are two solutions:

1. Manually look up relations in `version.revision.version_set.all()`. This is very tedious, and means abandoning the ORM.
2. Use the same transaction hack that the admin integration uses: https://github.com/etianen/django-reversion/blob/master/reversion/admin.py#L176

I'm planning on formalizing the admin transaction hack into a helper method at some point, but for the time being you need to duplicate the logic in your own codebase.

@yichen0831 ~

The parameter `obj` of the constructor is not intended to be used by the user. It is used in pyroaring to create a BitMap from a pointer to a `roaring_bitmap_t` (for instance in https://github.com/Ezibenroc/PyRoaringBitMap/blob/master/pyroaring.py#L105).
In a language like Java or C++, I would have used a private constructor for this.

I added a verification in the constructor to check for your example: https://github.com/Ezibenroc/PyRoaringBitMap/commit/36fc9882004c9b8ab02dcece5fc68441ce9bf4ea
Your example now raises an exception. To produce the same bug, you would have to call `BitMap(None, 4)` or `BitMap(obj=4)` which I think is unlikely. I am note sure there is a solution that will always prevent such bug (even if the constructor was safe, you could still overwrite the attribute `__obj__` of the BitMap).

I should write a documentation for pyroaring to prevent such issues.

No, the CNN model and the HOG models you are talking about are different ways of *detecting* faces (finding faces in a photo). They aren't face recognition models.

There's only one face recognition model in this library. No matter how you detect the faces in the image, the actual face recognition step (i.e. the `face_encodings()` function and then the `compare_faces()` function) is always done with a CNN. But that face encoding CNN model is totally separate from the face detection CNN or face detection HOG model you are talking about.

So that's why your question is confusing. There's not two different face recognition models to choose between. There's just the one. The only choice between models is for the face *detection* step.

Another option is available for this issue with the new 3.9.1 release. You could build your own decorator instead of using the built in jwt_required decorator which checks if an environmental variable (or setting or whatever) is set, and if so don't do any token verification and store some dummy data in the context stack (ex:`ctx_stack.top.jwt = jwt_data` or `ctx_stack.top.jwt_user = sqlalchemy_user`). The context stack is important, as it is what various helper functions like like jwt_identity and get_jwt_claims working.

Messing with the context stack internals obviously isn't the most ideal solution, as if there are ever any changes to this library there is no guarantee that those will still work as expected. But this does provide a way to start using this functionality right now. Maybe I could expose a `set_jwt_token` and `set_jwt_user` or something to provide a stable API for modifying the context stack with ones own decorators 🤷‍♂️ 

Some docs on getting started with building your own decorators can been seen here: http://flask-jwt-extended.readthedocs.io/en/latest/custom_decorators.html

from threading import Thread
from socketIO_client import SocketIO

host = 'localhost'
port = 5000


class TwoWayClient(object):
    def on_event(self, event):
        print event

    def __init__(self):
        self.socketio = SocketIO(host, port)
        self.socketio.on('event', self.on_event)

        self.receive_events_thread = Thread(target=self._receive_events_thread)
        self.receive_events_thread.daemon = True
        self.receive_events_thread.start()

        while True:
            some_input = raw_input()
            self.socketio.emit('event', some_input)

    def _receive_events_thread(self):
        self.socketio.wait()


def main():
    TwoWayClient()


if __name__ == "__main__":

Yes, each request has various objects associated with it (instances of HTTP protocol classes, Agent, the Deferred you are waiting on, buffers, etc.).  If you create an unbounded number of them you risk consuming an unbounded amount of memory.  You can avoid this, to some extent, by limiting the number of requests concurrently in flight. Despite receipts often called invoices too, both documents are significantly different:
* invoice is list of products within the legal information about merchant, which is requesting payment from the client;
* receipt is confirmation, that products or services has been paid.

Since typically ecommerce projects have instant payment (credit card, PayPal etc) it does make sense to issue and send receipt by default and allow developer to implement invoice generation for the project purposes (payment method via wire transfer, custom accounting functionality etc).

For those people starting `brand-new` project with oscar, I think [Saleor](https://saleor.readthedocs.io/en/latest/architecture/products.html) is giving a good example for variants scenarios.

With below alignments in `django-oscar`
* No more  standalone structures
* `ProductClass`'s attributes starting with `v_` exclusive for `Child` products, other `non-v` attributes for common attributes for all `Parent` products
* `Parent` products only get `non-v` attributes filled
* `Child` products only get `v_` attributes filled 
* Front can build sth similar to @john-parton 's `_get_variant_descriptors` on `v_` attributes only

I've pushed a change that includes the exact UPC matching with `exists` logic.

I understand that the code isn't obvious. Often, I will write the SQL to generate a query by hand, and then go back and write the Python to get Django's ORM to dance. This sometimes results in code that doesn't appear to be idiomatic Python/Django, because Django encourages JOINs over subqueries.

Let me know if you want me to make any other changes.

If you look at [the backend gRPC calls](https://developers.google.com/assistant/sdk/reference/rpc/google.assistant.embedded.v1alpha2), you can see that each `[AssistRequest](https://developers.google.com/assistant/sdk/reference/rpc/google.assistant.embedded.v1alpha2#google.assistant.embedded.v1alpha2.AssistRequest)` will contain an `[AssistConfig](https://developers.google.com/assistant/sdk/reference/rpc/google.assistant.embedded.v1alpha2#google.assistant.embedded.v1alpha2.AssistConfig)` optionally with `audio_in` packets. In the `AssistConfig`, you can see it accepts a union of either `AudioInConfig` or a text query.

It may be helpful to take a look at the source code for `[pushtotalk.py](https://github.com/googlesamples/assistant-sdk-python/blob/master/google-assistant-sdk/googlesamples/assistant/grpc/pushtotalk.py)` and `[textinput.py](https://github.com/googlesamples/assistant-sdk-python/blob/master/google-assistant-sdk/googlesamples/assistant/grpc/textinput.py)` to see how each makes API calls, then try to merge functionality from both.

Reading deisctl source, maybe the problem is:

When Stop calls [`expandTargets`](https://github.com/slack/deis/blob/3794474cbc4408a9c47a782b9a063230ffa90488/deisctl/backend/fleet/stop.go#L14), registry has the suffix `@*`, so it calls [`expandTarget` with `registry` argument](https://github.com/slack/deis/blob/3794474cbc4408a9c47a782b9a063230ffa90488/deisctl/backend/fleet/utils.go#L93) and **probably** [`fleetctl client`](https://github.com/slack/deis/blob/3794474cbc4408a9c47a782b9a063230ffa90488/deisctl/backend/fleet/utils.go#L107) make a simple grep with `registry` string only.

My suggestion: `expandTarget` should use a regex like [this](https://github.com/slack/deis/blob/3794474cbc4408a9c47a782b9a063230ffa90488/deisctl/backend/fleet/utils.go#L54) to filter `c.Units(target)` results.

In the `DistriOptimizer`, the dataset is type ` DistributedDataSet[MiniBatch[T]]`. `dataset.size()` calls `dataset.count`, defined as follows

````scala
buffer.mapPartitions(iter => {
    require(iter.hasNext)
    val array = iter.next()
    require(!iter.hasNext)
    Iterator.single(array.length)
  }).reduce(_ + _)
````

`buffer` here, is of type `RDD[Array[Minibatch[T]]]`, so `iter.next()` will produce `Array[Minibatch[T]]`. The length of that array will be the number of minibatches in that partition, and the sum of that across partitions will be the total number of minibatches in the dataset, not the total number of samples contained in all those minibatches.

Please also see `DistriOptimizerSpec` for further evidence, where the dataset used for tests defines the size method to be `256 * nodeNumber` which is the number of _minibatches_ in the dataset. There are 1024 minibatches in that dataset, but if you run the tests you will see that it only processes 32 minibatches per epoch, instead of 1024. It prints 

````
2017-07-24 08:24:31 INFO  DistriOptimizer$:295 - [Epoch 3 32/1024][Iteration 66][Wall Clock 3.874609356s] Train 32 in 0.045870153seconds.
````

But 32 is the number of _samples_ (accumulateCount), and 1024 is the number of _minibatches_ (dataset.size()). I ran into this issue because it also happened to me when I was running a normal training workload. The code used there is:

````scala
val trainRDD: RDD[MiniBatch[Float]] = ...
val train = DataSet.rdd(trainRDD)
val optimizer = Optimizer(model, train, new ClassNLLCriterion[Float]())
````

This produces the same problem of not processing all minibatches.

By strong regularization you mean evaluation on a set of users who have been completely excluded from training? This is not straightforward, but is still eminently doable.

You'll need to do the following:
1. Call fit with an interaction matrix that's oversized: say, it has 10,000 rows, but you only have training data for the first 8,000 customers (that is, all the entries in the last 2,000 rows are empty).
2. Freeze the gradients using the method we discussed above.
3. If you're using regularization, you may have to [reinitialize](https://github.com/lyst/lightfm/blob/master/lightfm/lightfm.py#L261) the last 2,000 rows of embeddings to make sure they haven't been regularized to zero.
4. Call `fit_partial` with a 10,000 row matrix: but this time, there will be no data in the first 8,000 rows - only data in the last 2,000 rows.

I'm with Andrew here, your test should be improved. You fire off a task (i.e. an asyncio thread) but don't wait for it. Then that task raises an exception (this is that pytest.fail() does, basically), but there's nothing to receive this exception.

That's not how pytest works. Your test will fail if your test function raises an exception, not if any task raises it.

Imagine a scenario with no asyncio, just using threads.

```
def test_thread():
    def failure():
        pytest.fail()
    
    import time
    from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
    t = ThreadPoolExecutor()
    f = t.submit(failure)
    time.sleep(1)
```

This won't fail either, unless you add `f.result()` at the end. An exception was raised in a separate thread, so what?

There is a very limited number of cases in which BrotliDecompressedSize works.
1) The whole input is compressed in a single block
2) The whole input is a single uncompressed block

The length of both compressed and uncompressed block is limited to 1 << 24 == 16MiB.

Technically it is possible to make this function work for a case: multiple uncompressed blocks followed by 0 or 1 compressed block.

Thanks!  The Cython in the _gate_to_grid_map.pyx file  is quite optimized, I don't expect that it can be improved much. Nearly all the computational time is spent in the `map_gates_to_grid` and `map_gate` methods which are almost complete typed and generating pure C code.

 A good method for finding places to improve Cython code is to look at the annotated html file generated by the command `cython -a _gate_to_grid_map.pyx`. Line highlighted in yellow are good candidates where adding type information may result in speed increases.  As can be see for the [_gate_to_grid_map.html](https://cdn.rawgit.com/jjhelmus/ff5ced43b1d0d1433efe/raw/00c57f0129996287fc07480b773fe68d3d80f1ce/_gate_to_grid_map.html) the entirety of `map_gate` method is free of yellow as is most of the `map_gates_to_grid`.

You may be interested in a talk I gave recently on optimizing Python and Cython code, slides and a video of the talk are available on the [conference website](https://sea.ucar.edu/event/profiling-python-code-improve-memory-usage-and-execution-time).  I'll be giving another presentation on Cython at the end of the month at [DePy](http://mdp.cdm.depaul.edu/DePy2015/default/schedule).  I'll post the slides and links from that presentation in my [posters repo](https://github.com/jjhelmus/posters)

>> Okay I think the bigger picture question is, should we let raw() be
>> default behavior? My vote is apparently No because otherwise I'd have not
>> even bothered to make it happen otherwise.
>>
>> To be more clear: conceptually, input: $input, parameter: value and $output:
>> output are different, and do not have to be consistent. That is,
>>
>>    1. "input"'s value always starts with $ to indicate output from
>>    another module;
>>    2. "output"'s value always takes a script variable, that is, raw() by
>>    default;
>>    3. "parameter"'s value will always add quotes to unquoted string. Any
>>    script variables that tries to go into "parameter" must use raw. This
>>    is to make a clear case between constants (specified strings and numbers)
>>    vs. script variables. If we quote strings, it kind of make them stand out
>>    from numbers, and unquoted script variables will go un-noticed. This is why
>>    I dislike it.
>>
>> I think this logic is clear. I may have failed to document them well,

However fish is apparently [not POSIX compliant](https://github.com/fish-shell/fish-shell/wiki/POSIX-compatibility-thoughts), which means you shouldn't expect to use it as /bin/sh and have everything work.

In case anyone comes here wanting to change their email address manually, I was able to do this by abusing the official client (tested on the test server).  The procedure:

1. Complete the instructions in [Running a local copy of the client](https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/contributing.html#running-a-local-copy-of-the-client).
2. Temporarily grant the current user permissions on `/etc/letsencrypt` and `/var/log/letsencrypt`.
3. In `letsencrypt/cli.py`, in `obtain_cert`, just after `le_client = _init_le_client(...)`, insert `import pdb; pdb.set_trace()`.
4. Run: `letsencrypt certonly --standalone`
5. When you hit the breakpoint, run: `le_client.acme.update_registration(le_client.account.regr, client.messages.Registration.from_data(email='NEW_EMAIL_ADDRESS'))`.  Ignore the `UnexpectedUpdate` error.
6. Run `le_client.acme.query_registration(le_client.account.regr)` and make sure it shows the new email address.
7. Press Ctrl-D.  The debugger will exit without completing the original command.
8. Change the permissions back.

With a little more effort, this could probably be turned into a first-draft Python script, but I've spent enough time on it for now.

That is the intended behavior change for dateutil version 2.5.2 - the combination of `dayfirst` and `yearfirst` are used to disambiguate between `ymd`, `ydm`, `dmy`, `mdy` as the "default" order for ambiguous triplets. See #81 and #238 for more details on how these work. At some point relatively soon, I will update the documentation with the chart in #238.

> It isn't obvious to newcomers that .replace(tzinfo=xxx) does the same as localize for pytz, and the internet is full with pytz examples. 

That's because it doesn't.  They do two different things, and `pytz` also has a `normalize` method that is unnecessary in `dateutil`. The function I wrote just *looks* like it does the same thing, but all it does is create an aware datetime. I'm not interested in supporting `pytz`'s non-standard API, nor making internet examples that  "just work" with `pytz` work with `dateutil`.

One thing to note is that while this is not guaranteed (because neither project makes any guarantees with respect to the other), `dateutil.tz.gettz(str(TZ))` will work for any `pytz` time zone generated by `hypothesis.extras.pytz.timezones` (so, basically, all the `pytz` zones), so you can always just convert your `pytz` time zones "at the border" to `dateutil.tz` zones, then use the standard Python time zone interface.

from notebook.utils import url_path_join


class MyServerExtensionHandler(IPythonHandler):
    def get(self):
        self.finish(self.render_template("my_serverextension/index.html"
                                         **self.application.settings))


def load_jupyter_server_extension(nb_app):
    base_url = nb_app.web_app.settings["base_url"]

    nb_app.web_app.settings["jinja2_env"].loader.searchpath += [
        join(dirname(__file__), "templates")
    ]

    nb_app.web_app.add_handlers(".*$", [
        (url_path_join(base_url, "my_serverextension"),
            MyServerExtensionHandler),
        ])
```

this assumes a structure like:
```shell
- my_serverextension/
  - __init__.py
  - templates/
    - my_serverextension/
      - index.html
```

This will not _overload_ the base templates, so if you are trying to change, say, `notebook`, that won't work. However, if you are adding rendered html handlers, it will work fine.

I don't know if adding it to the metadata is a feasible idea, as we don't actually interrogate the modules at runtime. We'd need a new configuration place for it, I guess.

Further, there is the ever-tricky question of ordering: 4.2 stores the server extensions as a dict, so your extension basically has to be ready to handle whatever comes your way.

That sounds like what it's expected to do. When you run `jupyter notebook` and it opens a browser for you, it passes the token to that browser in the URL (actually it's a separate token which only works a single time). The browser uses the token to get a cookie, and a redirect means you can't see the token in the address bar.

When you open it in a second browser, it's not authenticated, so you see the login page.

If you right click on the Jupyter logo in the browser that is already authenticated, you can use 'copy link' to get a link which includes the token. Paste that into another browser, and it bypasses the login page.

The more usual way to implement the workaround you give would be to use
```python
agent_reports = {"total": lambda a:list(a.data)}
```
but they're functionally the same.

The problem is that the lambda in the original code is handing a reference to the agent's data list off to the `DataCollector`. The very same list is collected at every step - not a copy of the list, but the same object. The agent's `step()` method modifies that object, and those modifications are visible everywhere the object exists - including every collected record for the agent. You can see the essence of the problem with this code:
```python
In [1]: a = [1, 2, 3]
In [2]: b = []
In [3]: b.append(a)
In [4]: b
Out[4]: [[1, 2, 3]]
In [5]: a.append(5)
In [6]: b
Out[6]: [[1, 2, 3, 5]]

```
The "WORKING" code replaces Agent.data at every step, and so each record collected by the DataCollector contains a different list object. Making a copy with `list(a.data)` accomplishes the same thing while allowing you to modify the agent's `data` attribute.

It's probably worth considering changing the data collector to do a `copy.deepcopy` on the data it's given to avoid the need for the workaround. @jackiekazil?

```
*** Settings ***
Library    Selenium2Library

Suite Setup     Open browser  http://example.com    chrome
Suite Teardown  close all browsers

*** Test Cases ***
Passing test
    go to   http://www.google.com

    fail  bummer.

Run the following commands:

$ pybot --output rerun.xml --rerunfailed output.xml /tmp/junk.robot

This adds a limit on datatype case splits for recursive types only if quantifiers are present.  datatype already does something related in apply_sort_cnstr().  The new parameter thus adds no new cross-cutting dependencies.

(smt.final-check "datatype")
(smt.stats :time 0.17 :before-memory 1.72 :after-memory 1.81)
(tactic-exception "smt tactic failed to show goal to be sat/unsat")
unknown
```

This change (which I have made on top of z3-4.4.1, approximately) also passes all of Z3's tests.

The default value of the new parameter is 10, causing Z3 to terminate on rdp-min.smt2 after about 0.2 seconds, which seems like a reasonable amount of time to me on that example, and is the reason I chose 10.  However, Z3 will pass all of its current tests even with the parameter at 0, which inhibits all recursive type case splits in the presence of quantifiers.

The TL:DR is I am not in favor of doing away with, or making major changes to, `ModelChain`.  I'm sure it can be better implemented, and the documentation and tutorials could be greatly improved.

I can't comment on `ModelChain` vs. `LocalizedPVSystem`, having used the former some, and the latter not at all. But the underlying user story for `ModelChain` is still valid: As a PV system modeler I want to run a sequence of models, specifying a model choice at each step, to get predicted PV system output. Before the `ModelChain` class we asked users to create scripts with the sequence of their chosen models. `ModelChain` provides some assistance with that process, and makes the scripting easier (I think) for new users.

One goal of PVLib (besides providing reference implementations of individual models) is to guide modelers through the modeling steps, from irradiance to AC power, and to educate about the choices available at each step. I think the current `ModelChain` meets this goal although in a somewhat less-user-friendly manner than I'd like.

You always need ``return_value_policy::reference_internal`` for what you are doing here -- the binding code is invalid as specified above. Because you're not specifying a return value policy, pybind11 activates an optimization which makes no sense in this setting: it checks if the returned address reference already exists as some kind of referenced Python object. Since the address is the same as the ``Interval`` instance, it avoids the cost of casting and directly returns that instance, which doesn't even have the right type. However, the problem is squarely with the binding code here.

@aldanor What makes `accessor` different is, I think, that it's a class designed to be an interface into attribute/container manipulation, *not* a wrapper around an attribute value.  When you do `obj = otherobj;` with ordinary `py::object`s you essentially make `obj` reference whatever `otherobj` references, but don't change whatever `obj` (used to) point to.  That's quite different from `accessor`, however: its copy assignment is really about setting an underlying value, not about referencing a new value—and that makes it different enough from `object` that I'm not sure it's conceptually suitable to be an `object` subclass.  It feels a bit weird to me to have `attr::operator=(const object &)` mean something fundamentally different from `object::operator=(const object &)`.

Now that said, part of this is really coming about because of, as you mention, the triple-purpose: one of those three purposes really *is* suitable to be an object subclass when all you are doing is accessing the attribute (which is, quite frankly, the most common case).  So perhaps another alternative to consider is to separate the accessor object from the manipulation object, something like:

```C++
object.getattr("name"); // Returns a new (eager) object-derived attribute subclass
object.attr("name"); // Returns the attribute manipulator, as occurs now
```

though I think this would really just be a shortcut to `object.attr("name").operator object()`—or perhaps a modified version that throws for non-existent attributes.

This looks reasonable to me.  A couple comment:

- `load_bytes` is obviously pointless when `sizeof(CharT) > 1`.  Is `PyUnicode_FromObject` conversely pointless when `sizeof(CharT) == 1`?  I'm not 100% sure, but I *think* it is, in which case it might be clearer to write to code as: `if (sizeof(CharT) == 1) { if (load_bytes(...)) return true; } else { PyUnicode_FromObject(...) }` so that the compiler can optimize away the dead code path.  (It also makes the code self-documenting, rather than putting the "else" logic inside a comment).
- the current code calls `PYBIND11_BYTES_CHECK` (i.e. `PyBytes_Check`) twice—once immediately before then again inside `load_bytes()`.  Since `load_bytes()` is going to succeed in the common case, I think you could clean up the logic slightly more to have something like: `if (sizeof(CharT) == 1 && load_bytes(...)) return true; else if (sizeof(CharT) > 1 && PYBIND11_BYTES_CHECK(...)) { ... } else { return false }`
- 💩 seems a totally apt unicode character choice for code dealing with unicode encoding issues under Python 2 :)

```

Using `/usr/local` __may__ require elevated permissions as it's a system-wide install.
For example of Ubuntu 14:04, I'm `ubuntu` and `/usr/local` is owned by `root` so `sudo` is needed.
```
ubuntu@azure-cli-dev3:~$ id -a
uid=1000(ubuntu) gid=1000(ubuntu) groups=1000(ubuntu)
ubuntu@azure-cli-dev3:~$ ls -ld /usr/local
drwxr-xr-x 10 root root 4096 Jul 14 20:41 /usr/local
```

As another example, on this particular OS X machine I am `derek` and `/usr/local` is owned by `derek` so `sudo` is not needed for global installation.
```
dereks-mbp:~ derek$ id
uid=501(derek)
dereks-mbp:~ derek$ ls -ld /usr/local
drwxrwxr-x  20 derek  admin  680 Aug  3 12:04 /usr/local
```

However, the ABC classes were moved in `collections.abc` in 3.3, before there was in `collections` directly. It's still the case for Python 2.7, it's why the code of `models` do:
```python
if sys.version_info < (3,):
    from collections import Iterable
    _unicode_type = unicode
else:
    from collections.abc import Iterable
    _unicode_type = str
```

If you look at the code in Python 3.5, `from collections import Iterable` still work
https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/3.5/Lib/collections/__init__.py
```python
# For backwards compatibility, continue to make the collections ABCs
# available through the collections module.
from _collections_abc import *
import _collections_abc
__all__ += _collections_abc.__all__
```

Maybe a more compatible way should be to try:
```
try: # Python 3.4 and after
   from collections.abc import Iterable
except ImportError: # Works for Python 2.7 and maybe some other interpreter
   from collections import Iterable
```

Some notes because in a year or whatever I'll probably look back and this and want to remember:

I talked about this at the language summit, and talked to Yury about it afterwards ([slides](https://vorpus.org/~njs/misc/trio-language-summit-2017.pdf)). The more ambitious proposal in those slides is similar but not identical to one in the thread up above:
* leave `await foo` alone
* make `await foo(*args, **kwargs)` sugar for:
  ```python
  if hasattr(type(foo), "__acall__"):
      awaitable = type(foo).__acall__(foo, *args, **kwargs)
  else:
      awaitable = foo(*args, **kwargs)
  return await awaitable
  ```
* trio can use a `@decorator` (or maybe eventually a `__future__`) to make its async functions provide `__awaitcall__` but give an error on `__call__`
* asyncio use a `@decorator` to make its async functions provide an `__awaitcall__` that returns the coroutine object (so `await foo()` is fast), an a `__call__` that returns a `Task` (so people don't need to worry about calling `ensure_future` and stuff, you can think of async functions as being things that return `Future`s when called normally, which is just like how traditional APIs worked. And now async functions and traditional `Future`-returning functions can be used in exactly the same ways, and `await foo()` and `foo().add_callback(...)` are maximally efficient regardless of how `foo` is implemented)
* we'd also of course need to update `functools.partial` to accept both `__acall__` and `__call__` and pass them through

See, it's cleverly designed to have something for everyone!

As I understood it, Yury had two main objections to this:

1. `asyncio` users don't really want async functions to return `Task`s, they actually just want bare coroutine objects that they can pass to `asyncio.gather` or whatever. ...on further thought this doesn't make a lot of sense to me though, because `asyncio.gather` will just wrap any any bare coroutine objects in `Task`s anyway, so my proposal doesn't hurt anything. Likely I misunderstood something. (Or maybe he just meant that this would only be a minor improvement for asyncio rather than a major one. I personally definitely find it confusing that asyncio has at least 3 different kinds of async functions – ones that return `Future`s, ones that return coroutine objects, and ones that return `Future`s but that are documented as returning coroutine objects because they want to reserve the right to change them in the future – and collapsing these down to just have one type of async function seems like a win to me.)

2. More importantly, he's concerned that implementing this would be really difficult, because (a) modifying the parser is annoying, (b) there are a whole bunch of bytecodes for calling functions (with or without `*` or `**` args, an optimized `CALL_METHOD`, etc. etc.), and this adds a whole new type of calling operation, so we'd need to double the number of call opcodes and duplicate a bunch of code and ugh.

So if/when someone decides to take this up again, I think these are the main things that would need answers.

I guess the obvious thing to try to resolve objection (2) would be the `RESOLVE_AWAITCALL` opcode suggested [up-thread](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/79#issuecomment-286269597). I'm not sure if Yury has some reason in mind why it wouldn't work.

Yeah, I know it can be simplified and improved. The goal was to quickly get a minimal example that reproduces the issue.

I agree, the core of the problem is actually not related to pytest at all but a result of the way that exceptions and nurseries work. If you have a nursery that starts two tasks, and the interaction of these tasks results in an exception being raised in each class, then only one exception is guaranteed to make it out of the nursery. The second one has to race against the `Cancelled` exception that's raised by the nursery. 

That said, why wouldn't it be feasible to introduce an optional grace period for task cancellation - at least in the context of testing? In other words, if one task terminates with exception, wait up to TIMEOUT seconds for other tasks to raise exceptions too, and only afterwards send the cancellation request. There is no good value for `TIMEOUT`, so it should default to zero, but this would at least allow adjusting the timeout if one gets testing failures that don't make sense. I believe if this isn't implemented in trio proper, it would require some rather nasty monkeypatching to achieve, so having it upstream would be nice.

I have a hypothesis.

```diff
+    register('--runtime-deps', advanced=True, type=list, member_type=target_option, fingerprint=True,
+             default=['//:avro-java-runtime'],
+             help='A list of specs pointing to dependencies of Avro generated code.')
```
Making this option a target_option means that the task fingerprinting will attempt to load the targets so that they can be fingerprinted. Something is failing when trying to do that. You could try leaving off the member type and seeing whether that works.

If it works, then that means that the target_option member type is the issue.

>
> How to produce:
>
> python_library(name='py2_only', compatibility=['CPython<3'])
>
> python_library(name='py3_only', compatibility=['CPython>=3'])
>
> python_binary(name='mytest',
>     source='mytest.py',
>     dependencies=[ intransitive(':py2_only'), ':py3_only'],
> )
>
> FAILURE: Unable to detect a suitable interpreter for compatibilities:
> CPython<3 && CPython>=3 && CPython<3,CPython>=3 (Conflicting targets:
> pkg:mytest, pkg:py2_only, pkg:py3_only)
>
> Would a PR be helpful here, or the current behavior is intentional/not a
> bug?
>
> Context on what I'm trying to do: In our current (non-pants) system, we
> have a package called base which is deployed to our pypi twice: as a
> python2-only wheel and as a python3-only wheel. The difference is that the
> Python 3 version contains a few additional sources that are using Python 3
> specific syntax (like yield from) so a universal package is impossible
> (Python 2 would choke when parsing this file).
>
> On top of that, we have some packages (type1) that are universal (the
> same sources support both Python 2 & Python 3) and depend on the base
> mentioned above. When a binary/test depends on type1 we know that the
> right version of base will be transitively picked up by pip.
>
> We also have a package type2, that like base, has distinct files for
> Python 2 and Python 3 versions and both of these versions depend on base.
> When a binary/test depends on type2 we know that the right version of base
> will be transitively picked up by pip.
>
> In pants, I thought to accomplish this by creating a python_aggregation
> target, like this:
>
> python_library(
>    name='base',
>    dependencies=[intransitive(':base_py2'), intransitive(':base_py3')]
> )
>
> and create a task that runs at bootstrap (similarly to
> substitute_aliased_targets) that would "hotwire" the right dependency based
> on the compatibility requirements of the binary/test being run. Thoughts?
>

_2nd NN determination_: I think you make a good point about avoiding repeatedly calculating the first nearest neighbors. I'll starting to work on that code soon, and I'll tag you on the PR in case you want to critique it.

_Neighboring Site Featurizer_: Something like SiteStatsFeaturizer could work for that kind of feature. I'll keep that use case in mind when writing the 2nd NN code, so that you could use it effectively.

_Caching_: I like your idea. After thinking some more, here are the options I'm considering.

1. Introducing a utility function to matminer that takes the NearNeighbors class, structure, and site number as input and uses `lru_cache`/`joblib.Memory` to serve repeated calls. We would call that rather than the NearestNeighbor operations directly.
  - Multiple featurizers would call the same ultility function. And, if someone uses `MultiFeaturizer`, the access pattern will result in plenty of cache hits.
  - Problem: Not sure how burdensome calling the utility function rather than pymatgen directly would be. 
  - Problem: `lru_cache` will not natively recognize if you pass two different instantiations of the same NearNeighbor class as the same. To do so, we'd need to implement `__eq__` and `__hash__` for each NearNeighbor class.
2. Storing the NN information as an attribute of the Structure object, and our featurizers would check for the presence of that data before re-computing it. (I'd write also write a utility function for each of these)
  - This is how I cache tessellations in Magpie
  - Problem: Could run into problems with memory for larger datasets (I did with Magpie). We would need to write code that clears out the NN attributes after they are no longer useful.

I lean option 1. I don't think using a utility operation is a big deal and, while I recognize implementing `__eq__` and `__hash__` introduces a vector for difficult-to-debug errors, I do not see it as too burdensome to police. Any thoughts/other ideas?

Thing is that although the no test fail, this patch does change semantics of ValidationError arguments (the same way the original PR does, although it also requires to specify fields names which make it even more ridiculous and convoluted). Accompanied by abusing lack of good test coverage this PR does indeed pass all tests (the `fields_names` argument in ValidationError was supposed to mean that THE SAME error applies to all arguments listed. This PR changes that to "messages dictionary contains errors for specified field names" which does not play well if you want to report the same error, which is a dictionary, to multiple fields). So, it's backward incompatible. And if to choose between two backward incompatible PRs, I would choose the first one: it's more complete, exposes useful helper functions/classes for building errors, IT ADDS TESTS.

When you say 'source a path' what is actually happening is the following:

1. A bash script (in this case, `/etc/profile`) is executed
2. This bash script can do many many things, including setting environment
variables  (with `export`)

So `/etc/profile` can do plenty of things, including setting environment
variables. However, this flexibility means it could produce hard to debug
errors sometimes - and hence the more explicit method of setting
`c.SystemdSpawner.environment` is preferred.

Hope that answers your question!

NE3 is very exciting to me. I am in cognitive science and do a lot of computationalmodeling. I recently made a change of my modeling language from Matlab to Python. In this process, NE2 has been an indispensable tool. I tried Numba but found NE2 generally much faster for the type of computation I do. But I found that there are some limitations of NE2 that would prevent my code from running even faster:

- Without multi-line support, even some simple algorithms cannot be compiled and evaluated in a single call.
- Some functions that I need such as the error function family are not supported.
- Setup time for threading increases with the increase of CPU cores, which in turn increases the break-even point (this forces me to set the # of cores to a number less than available cores for some appllication).

In my opinion these kinds of problems are easiest to capture by using the existing tests, but changing the case-sensitive fields (`username` and `password`, others?) to include values which use uppercase letters.

So for example [tests/influxdb/influxdb08/client_test.py#L102](https://github.com/influxdb/influxdb-python/blob/master/tests/influxdb/influxdb08/client_test.py#L102) would change from
`cli = InfluxDBClient.from_DSN('influxdb://usr:pwd@host:1886/db')`
to
`cli = InfluxDBClient.from_DSN('influxdb://uSr:pWd@host:1886/db')`

Also, for easier change (and avoiding typos) the "base url" should be one variable, instead of rewriting it all the time (e.g. `DSN_STRING = 'influxdb://uSr:pWd@host:1886/db'`). This way the same "base url" could be used in all the cases, just by using `'udp+' + DSN_STRING` for UDP-test etc.

Pour openfisca, le seul endroit où, me semble-t-il, cela est pertinent ce sont les prestations sociales.
Mais elles sont souvent mensuelles et débutent à partir du mois civil qui suit l'anniversaire (pour les AF par exemple). Cela explique peut-être que nous n'ayons pas vu émerger ce problème plus tôt.

Il y a bien un besoin de templatiser les formules openfisca, et le périmètre des cotisations sociales est sans doute celui où le besoin est le plus marqué.

À partir de là, tu as deux choix:

1. Tu restes dans le cadre openfisca, et tu en subis les contraintes. Tu serais amené à créer un nouveau concept de `Template`, avec un constructeur prenant en gros les mêmes entrées que ce que tu mets dans ton `yaml`, et qui serait capable de faire des calculs. Je pense que c'est relativement découplé du coeur, donc tu ne souffrirais pas trop de la dette, ni du vectoriel, mais ça restera du python.

2. Tu t'affranchis de toute contrainte, et tu essayes de construire ce qui te semble être la représentation idéale des règles de calculs, en essayant de rester *théoriquement* exécutable. Et idéalement, à la fin, tu arrives à faire en sorte que ce soit *effectivement* exécutable, par exemple en faisant un pont vers openfisca, ou en faisant ton propre moteur. Ne rêvons pas,  *"chacun est libre de développer son moteur"* mais personne autre que toi ne vas s'amuser à parser le language que tu auras créé.

Après, nous avons tous à l'incubateur la joie de choisir nos priorités, donc à toi de pondérer tes envies, les risques et la valeur ajoutée des deux scénarios.
