You will notice that the solution given by `eval` has the 6th byte as 0.

If you are expecting printable characters, you could do something similar to what was done in [this example](https://github.com/angr/angr-doc/blob/a4a92a74b404046dc3535c78d32273d74c25bc89/examples/google2016_unbreakable_0/solve.py#L24-L27).

Here is a detailed explanation: CFGFast relies on VEX, and VEX cannot decode some instructions inside this binary (e.g., `MRS.W   R0, CONTROL`). That directly causes a decoding error to happen (an IRSB with the jumpkind `Ijk_NoDecode`).

CFGFast was developed under the assumption that the lifter (decoder) is omnipotent and is able to decode *all* instructions. Unfortunately, this is not the case, and we will have to handle it gracefully.

What makes things worse is that when a function has a decoding error which stops us from reaching its returns, that function will be identified as non-returning, which totally breaks the call chains. This is why  
the `Main` function is not fully recognized in `CFGFast`.

Here are my proposed solutions:

- Loosening the assumption that the lifter can lift all instructions it comes across. Whenever there is a NoDecode error, we will consult capstone to get the length of that instruction, and skip that undecodeable instruction in lifting. In addition, if we have a conditional exit from the part of the IRSB that the lifter supports (i.e., there is a conditional exit before VEX reaches an unsupported instruction), we will happily take that exit target and continue CFG recovery from there.
- Treating functions with decoding errors in the middle as of an unknown returning status. This will confine decoding errors within the same function, without screwing up the entire calling stack from the entry point (or wherever your CFG recovery begins) to that function.

I may be a little biased, as I work on the dev team for the project, but I think that angr is an excellent choice for that. In order to do any sort of full-program analysis with the VEX IR, you're going to need a control flow graph that's at least a little bit smart (i.e. has any hope of resolving indirect jumps), which angr provides. Even though it's not 100% accurate, it's pretty good.

Data type recovery is an open field of research at the moment, but so far the best results available have been produced with static analysis tools like angr. It'll be much easier to talk IRL about these "strong disagreements", but here are some high-level musings as my input in the meantime:

- If we add a "runnable example" and skip it in tests, then chances are the example will become obsolete sooner or later... We saw this Murphy law happening several times already. Are we worried about it here in the records/pidstore use case? 

- Generally speaking, if `invenio-foo` does not depend on `invenio-bar` for its runtime operation (i.e. no dependency declared in `install_requires` or `extras`), then I think it is not good to depend on it in tests only (i.e. dependency declared in `tests_require` only). Isn't it surprising to mandate `invenio-foo` to be a sort of integration testing place for collaboration with `invenio-bar`, if it is a "lesser" package in the ecosystem, since the "higher" package is already testing this collaboration? 

- Such a "mutual testing" between P1 and P2 could get even more complicated if there are several inter-dependent packages P1, P2, P3, P4 in the game... What about using Invenio demo site as a natural inter-package integration testing place? A Jenkins style of nightly integration testing jobs comprising all our 80+ packages for all our 4+ flavour use cases would be happening there naturally, making sure all the packages work well together. We need to do this before getting to making "flavour releases" anyway.

- Whatever we choose, we'll probably need lots of inter-sphinx for closely related packages for the documentation to make sense to people bringing these independent packages together...

Consider the three possible choices pre-commit could make in this situation:

1. Don't look at modifications as a failure and continue the commit as normal:
    - This will cause commits of unvetted changes (and incorrect contents)
2. `git add` after changes and continue committing
    - What if the committer has unstaged changes on that file?  Attempting to restore them would break.
    - What if the git hook is incorrect? You've now committed broken things.
    - What if the git hook depends on the behaviour of others (for example, a rewriter that expects autopep8 to fix up some indentation)? Again, you've now committed "broken" things.
    - What if two rewriting hooks disagree on how a file should be formatted?  Suddenly you're switching formatting back and forth on every commit :)
3. Treat modifications as a "failure" and leave the intelligent end user to perform the correct actions of the above whether it's:
    - Accept the automatic modifications and `git add`
    - Adjust pre-commit hook settings and re-commit
    - Modify the changes made
    - etc.

If I understand correctly, this happens in a pipenv:

```
auto-load: Attempted file "/usr/lib/debug$HOME/.virtualenvs/pyProj-UoyQSP0Z/bin/python2-gdb.gdb" does not exist.
```

and this happens outside of a pipenv:

```
auto-load: Attempted file "/usr/lib/debug/usr/bin/python2.7-gdb.py" exists.
```

I assume that pipenv does not copy the necessary GDB scripts to the newly created virtualenvs and therefore GDB needs to load them from their original locations.

This can be achieved by using a symlinked Python executable from within a virtualenv. GDB uses full path to the executable, after following symlinks, to construct the path to the required GDB script. That is why you see GDB attempting to load a file from a strange path `/usr/lib/debug$HOME/.virtualenvs/pyProj-UoyQSP0Z/bin/python2-gdb.gdb` inside pipenv. If possible, configure pipenv so that symlinks, not copies, of the Python executables, are used.

If symlinks are not an option, you can copy the GDB scripts to the locations where GDB expects to find them. You may also modify `auto-load` `scripts-directory` and `auto-load` `safe-path` according to your needs. It would change the starting points where GDB looks for the scripts to `auto-load`. But the actual script paths would still be derived from the full path to the object file (in this case the Python executable). I am not aware of a way to change that behavior.

```
print t
```

I know this is old but for those who just want the backend I actually converted the main part of this script to boto3:

```python
from django.conf import settings
from django.core.mail.backends.base import BaseEmailBackend

from boto3.session import Session
import botocore

from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from time import sleep

settings.AWS_SES_AUTO_THROTTLE = getattr(settings, 'AWS_SES_AUTO_THROTTLE', 0.5)

settings.DKIM_DOMAIN = getattr(settings, "DKIM_DOMAIN", None)
settings.DKIM_PRIVATE_KEY = getattr(settings, 'DKIM_PRIVATE_KEY', None)
settings.DKIM_SELECTOR = getattr(settings, 'DKIM_SELECTOR', 'ses')
settings.DKIM_HEADERS = getattr(settings, 'DKIM_HEADERS',
            ('From', 'To', 'Cc', 'Subject'))

TIME_ZONE = settings.TIME_ZONE

# These would be nice to make class-level variables, but the backend is
# re-created for each outgoing email/batch.
# recent_send_times also is not going to work quite right if there are multiple
# email backends with different rate limits returned by SES, but that seems
# like it would be rare.
cached_rate_limits = {}
recent_send_times = []


def dkim_sign(message, dkim_domain=None, dkim_key=None, dkim_selector=None, dkim_headers=None):
    """Return signed email message if dkim package and settings are available."""
    try:
        import dkim
    except ImportError:
        pass
    else:
        if dkim_domain and dkim_key:
            sig = dkim.sign(message,
                            dkim_selector,
                            dkim_domain,
                            dkim_key,
                            include_headers=dkim_headers)
            message = sig + message
    return message


class SESBackend(BaseEmailBackend):
    """A Django Email backend that uses Amazon's Simple Email Service.
    """

    def __init__(self, fail_silently=False, **kwargs):

        super(SESBackend, self).__init__(fail_silently=fail_silently, **kwargs)
        
        self.session = Session(
            aws_access_key_id = settings.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID,
            aws_secret_access_key = settings.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY,
            region_name = settings.AWS_REGION_NAME
        )
        
        self._throttle = settings.AWS_SES_AUTO_THROTTLE

        self.dkim_domain = settings.DKIM_DOMAIN
        self.dkim_key = settings.DKIM_PRIVATE_KEY
        self.dkim_selector = settings.DKIM_SELECTOR
        self.dkim_headers = settings.DKIM_HEADERS

        self.connection = None

    def open(self):
        """Create a connection to the AWS API server. This can be reused for
        sending multiple emails.
        """
        if self.connection:
            return False

        try:
            self.connection = self.session.client('ses')
        except:
            if not self.fail_silently:
                raise

    def close(self):
        """Close any open HTTP connections to the API server.
        """
        try:
            #self.connection.close()
            self.connection = None
        except:
            if not self.fail_silently:
                raise
    def send_messages(self, email_messages):
        """Sends one or more EmailMessage objects and returns the number of
        email messages sent.
        """
        if not email_messages:
            return

        new_conn_created = self.open()
        if not self.connection:
            # Failed silently
            return

        num_sent = 0
        for message in email_messages:
            # Automatic throttling. Assumes that this is the only SES client
            # currently operating. The AWS_SES_AUTO_THROTTLE setting is a
            # factor to apply to the rate limit, with a default of 0.5 to stay
            # well below the actual SES throttle.
            # Set the setting to 0 or None to disable throttling.
            if self._throttle:
                global recent_send_times

                now = datetime.now()

                # Get and cache the current SES max-per-second rate limit
                # returned by the SES API.
                rate_limit = self.get_rate_limit()

                # Prune from recent_send_times anything more than a few seconds
                # ago. Even though SES reports a maximum per-second, the way
                # they enforce the limit may not be on a one-second window.
                # To be safe, we use a two-second window (but allow 2 times the
                # rate limit) and then also have a default rate limit factor of
                # 0.5 so that we really limit the one-second amount in two
                # seconds.
                window = 2.0  # seconds
                window_start = now - timedelta(seconds=window)
                new_send_times = []
                for time in recent_send_times:
                    if time > window_start:
                        new_send_times.append(time)
                recent_send_times = new_send_times

                # If the number of recent send times in the last 1/_throttle
                # seconds exceeds the rate limit, add a delay.
                # Since I'm not sure how Amazon determines at exactly what
                # point to throttle, better be safe than sorry and let in, say,
                # half of the allowed rate.
                if len(new_send_times) > rate_limit * window * self._throttle:
                    # Sleep the remainder of the window period.
                    delta = now - new_send_times[0]
                    total_seconds = (delta.microseconds + (delta.seconds +
                                     delta.days * 24 * 3600) * 10**6) / 10**6
                    delay = window - total_seconds
                    if delay > 0:
                        sleep(delay)

                recent_send_times.append(now)
                # end of throttling
            try:
                response = self.connection.send_raw_email(
                    Source = settings.DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL,
                    Destinations = message.recipients(),
                    RawMessage = { 'Data': dkim_sign(
                        message.message().as_string(),
                        dkim_key=self.dkim_key,
                        dkim_domain=self.dkim_domain,
                        dkim_selector=self.dkim_selector,
                        dkim_headers=self.dkim_headers
                    )}
                )
                
                message.extra_headers['status'] = response['ResponseMetadata']['HTTPStatusCode']
                message.extra_headers['message_id'] = response['MessageId']
                message.extra_headers['request_id'] = response['ResponseMetadata']['RequestId']
                num_sent += 1
            except botocore.exceptions.ClientError as error:
                # Store failure information so to post process it if required
                print(error)
                error_keys = ['status', 'reason', 'body', 'request_id',
                              'error_code', 'error_message']
                for key in error_keys:
                    message.extra_headers[key] = getattr(error, key, None)
                if not self.fail_silently:
                    raise

        if new_conn_created:
            self.close()

        return num_sent
    def get_rate_limit(self):
        if settings.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID in cached_rate_limits:
            return cached_rate_limits[settings.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID]

        new_conn_created = self.open()
        if not self.connection:
            raise Exception(
                "No connection is available to check current SES rate limit.")
        try:
            quota_dict = self.connection.get_send_quota()
            print(quota_dict)
            max_per_second = quota_dict['MaxSendRate']
            ret = float(max_per_second)
            cached_rate_limits[settings.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID] = ret
            return ret
        finally:
            if new_conn_created:
                self.close()
```

I checked some larger VCFs and unfortunately spotted a problem... All of the *AF fields are strictly floats, except for "AF" (1000G). 

In rare cases (always for INDELs as far as I can tell) this field will contain two values separated by an &. For example "0.0791&0.07905". The weird thing is that they appear to almost always be the same value, just with different precision (4 vs 5 decimal places). According to the documentation this should not happen, so I suppose it is a bug in VEP.

I will test if this is fixed in VEP 91, but at the moment this will most likely cause crashes when loading VCFs from VEP 90. I haven't had time to actually test, but float("0.0791&0.07905") will throw a ValueError.

```
    def function_resolver(self, operation_id):
        """Map the operation_id to the function in this class."""
        if '.' in operation_id:
            _, function_name = operation_id.rsplit('.', 1)
        else:
            function_name = operation_id
        function = getattr(self, function_name)
        return function

    def start_server(self):

        self.run()
```

> Please also notice this issue in FF42 / Windows 7:

Uhm... yeah. That could very well be because Firefox, when run in most VMs *disables* DirectWrite and falls back to GDI rendering. The default behavior of GDI rendering in Windows applications is to adher to the OS setting for ClearType. And in most VM installs it will be *turned off* by default.

Hence all the jagged edges in Firefox on both the icon font and the normal text.

IE though, while it will disable DirectWrite, will by default override the OS level setting for ClearType and will render with ClearType *enabled*, so in IE's case the normal text will be ok, and only the icon font will be jaggy because the icon font is not properly hinted.

Chrome will, when DirectWrite is not available, fall back to its own internal font renderer which, if I remember correctly, will render with full geometric precision when no hinting is present.


So.. I guess you just delivered the evidence you were looking for that proves the hinting is broken?

As I have said, the implementation is easy although I might have missed some corner cases, and we will see how to handle more complex workflows.

For example, the idea of `task` is to submit large tasks to task queues but you are essentially running everything in tasks, which can be really troublesome if the task queue is full. In practice, you might have to define two hosts, one for PBS/Slurm and one for head node, and run the smaller jobs directly on headnode as follows:

```
[10]
input: 'sos/target.py'
output: 'target.pyc'
task: queue='head-node'
sh:
    cp ${input} ${output}
```
Then the `-r -q slurm` option will submit these jobs to headnode and larger tasks to slurm. This is not particularly difficult to understand or implement though.

I have a much cleaner comparison at https://gist.github.com/stsievert/981ee42e70ccf81090aba37c3afe2bc0 with same model as in https://github.com/dask/dask-ml/pull/221#issuecomment-415239292: (this shows the results of the best estimator after the search below) In this, I find this comparison for different successive halving brackets of Hyperband: There's a definite advantage to certain brackets, but it's not super clear (there are many different lines). So here's a comparison with Hyperband (which is nothing more than all the different brackets). This shows a definite advantage of using Hyperband. An interesting observation that reinforces the debugging comment in https://github.com/dask/dask-ml/pull/221#issuecomment-415239292: in developing these plots, I had a bug where the same parameter set would be tested but with different seeds for the optimizer (see 4f8c14c1936eb98479fea1f0be4cd3ac144018de). The only difference between the models was training time then, and naturally passive random sampling out performed everything because it trained the most models to `max_iter`. There's no point in doing anything adaptive if training time is the only thing that matters in differentiating models, and the least adaptive bracket of Hyperband will perform the best. Likewise, if the validation loss if a deterministic function of the parameters (say with `ConstantFunction`) being more adaptive and doing more parameter exploration will be beneficial, so the most adaptive Hyperband bracket will perform the best.

When it is run with `orientations=2`, as above, the HOG function groups gradients into two bins:
* one bin contains gradient vectors lying in the (+x, +y) and (-x, -y) quadrants

and the the dot version of `_upsampled_dft` (the relevant part):

```python
    elif data.ndim == 3:
        upsampled_region_size = np.asarray(upsampled_region_size, dtype=int)
        im2pi = -1j * 2 * np.pi
        plane_kernel = np.exp(
            (-im2pi / (data.shape[0] * upsample_factor)) *
            (np.arange(upsampled_region_size[0])[:, None] - axis_offsets[0]).dot(
            (np.fft.ifftshift(np.arange(data.shape[0]))[None, :] - np.floor(data.shape[0] / 2)))
        )
        row_kernel = np.exp(
            (-im2pi / (data.shape[1] * upsample_factor)) *
            (np.arange(upsampled_region_size[1])[:, None] - axis_offsets[1]).dot(
            (np.fft.ifftshift(np.arange(data.shape[1]))[None, :] - np.floor(data.shape[1] / 2)))
        )
        col_kernel = np.exp(
            (-im2pi / (data.shape[2] * upsample_factor)) *
            (np.arange(upsampled_region_size[2])[:, None] - axis_offsets[2]).dot(
            (np.fft.ifftshift(np.arange(data.shape[2]))[None, :] - np.floor(data.shape[2] / 2)))
        )
        out = np.reshape(
            plane_kernel.dot(np.reshape(data, (data.shape[0], -1))),
            (upsampled_region_size[0], data.shape[1], data.shape[2]))
        out = np.transpose(out, (1, 0, 2))
        out = np.reshape(
            row_kernel.dot(np.reshape(out, (data.shape[1], -1))),
            (upsampled_region_size[1], upsampled_region_size[0], data.shape[2]))
        out = np.transpose(out, (1, 0, 2))
        out = np.transpose(out, (2, 1, 0))
        out = np.reshape(
            col_kernel.dot(np.reshape(out, (data.shape[2], -1))),
            upsampled_region_size)
        out = np.transpose(out, (2, 1, 0))
        return out
```

I've been playing with a solution to the auth issue over the weekend. Basically the flow becomes:

1. Go to https://auth.mopidy.com/spotify
2. Get redirected to Spotify and accept the "Spotify extension for Mopidy" app
3. Get sent back https://auth.mopidy.com/spotify/callback which will:
  1. Complete the oauth flow getting an `auth_token` + `refresh_token`
  2. Generate a `client_id` + `client_secret` for the extension
  3. Encrypt the tokens using the new secret
  4. Store just the `client_id` + encrypted tokens
  5. Respond with a the new `client_id` and `client_secret`
4. User puts these credentials in the extension config
5. Extension can now use the `client_credentials` oauth flow to refresh against our service which proxies the spotify auth handling refreshes etc.

I had some other approaches initially, but all of them would up being weird hybrid flows that didn't match anything in the OAuth RFC. Among these was just giving the `refresh_token` to the user and not storing it ourselves and hosting a swap service. But this gets iffy if the service every gives us a new `refresh_token`.

For most of the other variants the security properties wound up being mostly the same. That is if someone gets your credentials or `refresh_token` they can keep getting new `auth_tokens` from our endpoint. However, with the `client_credentials` variant we can at least revoke (delete the encrypted blob) for a given client if anyone finds they want to reset things. As for us storing the tokens, we encrypt them using `Fernet` from cryptography.io so that they are useless to us unless the right client is asking for them.

As for running this, I have the code mostly done. But I'm planning to go over and add tests and do some other cleanups before launching this. This also doesn't exclude continuing to use the "direct model" if anyone doesn't want to trust our swap service, or run their own for that matter.

Correct, `deactivate` does not simply restore the `PATH` to the original. See lines ~164-180 in `deactivate.sh`, also reproduced below:

```
###########################################################################
# RESTORE PATH                                                            #
# remove only first instance of $CONDA_PREFIX from $PATH, since this is   #
# Unix we will only expect a single path that need to be removed, for     #
# simplicity and consistency with the Windows *.bat scripts we will use   #
# fuzzy matching (/f) to get all of the relevant removals                 #
PATH=$(envvar_cleanup.bash "${PATH}" --delim=":" -u -f "${CONDA_PREFIX}")
if [ $? != 0 ]; then
    [ "${IS_ENV_CONDA_VERBOSE}" = "${FALSE}" ] && unset CONDA_VERBOSE
    unset IS_ENV_CONDA_VERBOSE
    unset TRUE
    unset FALSE
    return 1
fi
export PATH
# END RESTORE PATH                                                        #
###########################################################################
```

Converting `$CONDA_PREFIX` into an array of sorts is an option but then again arrays are far from shell agnostic.

here I'd like to give back an error message

```
The search scope for '--reverse-dependency' must be limited to a single package.

---
Name                       Version                   Build  Channel
python                     3.6.4                hc167b69_0  defaults
python                     3.6.4                hc167b69_1  defaults
```

at which point the user could enter something like

    conda search python=3.6.4[build_number=1] --reverse-dependency

and get back a non-error result.

All the possible known packages in the currently configured list of channels that might be pulled in as a result of this single package?  That seems to be what the behavior is in 4.3.x. My apologies, I've found that this is _not_ expected behaviour. With the example I gave above, the insert will work and be committed, but the connection will not be closed when you expect it to be. Something like this will work:

    conn = sqlanydb.connect( ... )
    with conn as c:
        c.execute( 'insert into mytable...' )
    conn.close()

But this seems odd. It would make more sense to me to support:

    with sqlanydb.connect( ... ) as conn:
        curs = conn.cursor()
        curs.execute( 'insert into mytable...' )
        curs.close()
        # Other things using conn

Where the connection is closed at the end of the `with` statement. Let me know if I've misunderstood something, but it seems to me that the second example above would be the more intuitive behaviour.

> I do not use detox because I got strange errors when trying it on several projects (errors which do not happen when running plain tox.

Then those projects are broken 😄 There are some legit reasons why projects can't be tested in parallel (gevent is one---it spawns a number of servers that need to use fixed network ports, which obviously can conflict in parallel), but in my experience most often it's just poorly written tests that assume constant paths to temp files and such.

> Why/how does my change break on detox?

I guess "break" is a strong word. It's just pointless under detox. All the environments listed in `envlist` are run at the same time, so the coverage environment has nothing to report because everything else is still running. At best you get partial data, depending on exactly when 'coverage erase' and 'coverage combine' happen with respect to everything else.

I understand that this project has large swaths of code that are only run under either python 2 or python 3, so having one single environment produce the canonical coverage report is not really tractable. Luckily, Coverage 4.5 was [recently released](https://coverage.readthedocs.io/en/coverage-4.5.1/changes.html#version-4-5-2018-02-03), and its key feature [is designed to address exactly this situation](https://nedbatchelder.com/blog/201802/coverage_45.html), so that both Python 2 and Python 3 can run coverage, and Python 3 can explicitly say "ignore the Python 2 files" and vice versa, allowing each individual environment (theoretically) to have 100% coverage of its own relevant code.

Quick update for accuracy as a function of the temporal sampling (`time_step` kwarg):  I ran a single atmosphere for 30s with a time_step of 5ms and saved all the instantaneous PSFs.  (To increase the execution speed, I set oversampling=1.0 and max_size=3.0 arcsec.  This brings things down from ~3 hr to ~9 minutes on my laptop).  I then added together various subsets of the instantaneous images to mimic using different time_steps that are multiples of 5ms.  The following are plots of the 30 second PSF relative size and ellipticity deviations as a function of the time_step used, and where the reference "correct" PSF is the one computed using the 5ms time step.

![delta_rsqr](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3650485/15089776/7a2a5a5e-13c0-11e6-8610-adc486dd4c1d.png)

![delta_e](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3650485/15089777/80f38040-13c0-11e6-9d0c-fe6875d9f1f3.png)

![delta_e1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3650485/15089778/85bd3cf6-13c0-11e6-9cfd-2edddda6df72.png)

![delta_e2](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3650485/15089780/89ad0666-13c0-11e6-9d97-f19689e7592a.png)

So if, say, ~10^-3 accuracy is desired, then 50 ms intervals (10x speedup) looks likely sufficient.

More important than individual second moments though are things like 2 point correlation functions, which I'm now tooling up to start investigating.  Stay tuned.

Our original use case is to obtain different userids depending on scope (to isolate data between apps basically).
What if we just define two clients IDs and define two policies?

```ini
    multiauth.policy.notes.issuer = https://accounts.google.com
    multiauth.policy.notes.client_id = YYYYYYYY.apps.googleusercontent.com
    multiauth.policy.notes.client_secret = UAlL-YYYYYYYY

    multiauth.policy.lockbox.issuer = https://accounts.google.com

    multiauth.policy.lockbox.client_secret = UAlL-XXXXXX
```

RFC 6347 4.2.4:

> There are three ways to exit the WAITING state:
> (...)
> 2. The implementation reads a retransmitted flight from the peer: the
      implementation transitions to the SENDING state, where it
      retransmits the flight, resets the retransmit timer, and returns
      to the WAITING state.  The rationale here is that the receipt of a
      duplicate message is the likely result of timer expiry on the peer
      and therefore suggests that part of one's previous flight was
      lost.

It looks that this PR breaks this rule.
I thought you want to prepare for case when we get a few duplicated flights in rx buffer. It may happen when crypto calculations prevents OT from clearing rx buffer. @jvanasco  I appreciate you taking the time to think through this!

My thoughts below:

> 1- The clarification on HTML attributes looks good. It's not necessary due to the fixes PR, but a good idea (our issue was that we put "attribute-safe" data into bleach, but it was decoded then "cleaned" into an unsafe format.)

`bleach.clean()` output is still **not** safe for HTML attributes. You need to use your templating engine to escape the output of `bleach.clean()`. This PR doesn't fix that hence the additional documentation.

> 2- I'm worried by the loss of idempotent transforms. It doesn't affect us, but this has the potential to really break stuff. I know it may not be avoidable. IMHO this would need to be a 2.1 not 2.0.1 release with a big warning.

These changes are targeting a 2.1 release because it includes backwards-incompatible changes. That's covered in the changes in the `CHANGES` file. I think that covers your concerns.

Regarding idempotency, I'm not sure how sanitizing could ever be idempotent because of what's it's doing. There are notes in the first commit message about that.

If you think this is a necessary property for `bleach.clean`, I'm game for reading a compelling argument, but note that similar functions in other places aren't idempotent (escape, quote, and so on).

`bleach.linkify` is different. It could be idempotent since it's adding links to things.

> 3- The new encoding behavior will break installs where html entities are entered, which includes many wyswig markdown editors. ...

It seems like the use case here is that users use a wysiwig editor, type stuff in, and the editor adds additional things like bullet points using character entities. Then the system which can't differentiate between what the user wrote and what the editor added, runs all that text through `bleach.clean()` which will convert things like `•` to `&bull;`.

I'd love to know more about this use case. None of the major projects that use Bleach do this as far as I know.

My gut feeling is that what they're doing isn't a supported use case for Bleach and they're doing things to get around what Bleach does. That's fine, and you're right--these changes will probably break that. I'm not sure I think that's a bad thing or that it should stop us from landing this PR as is.

Do you have examples of systems like this so I can look at them?

Are there ways to fix the behavior we're fixing in this PR and also make that use case easier? It might be possible to consume entities specified in an allow list and leave the rest, but I think that creates new issues since `consumeEntity` can get called in a bunch of different contexts.

> 4- A tiny breaking change is how bleach handles the "special" cgi.escape entities if entered in the numeric form. previously these were transcoded from numeric to text.

This is covered in several of the tests.

Bleach is designed to take user-provided input and sanitize it for HTML output. If a user literally types in `&`, are they expecting that to be a whitespace character when rendered in a webpage? That seems really curious to me.

What's the use case where `&` ends up in the text and the expected behavior is that it comes out as whitespace?

> 5- The clean and linkify commands on query strings don't create the same output, still break links, and don't necessarily work as expected:

Your first two examples are actually correct behavior because the output of `bleach.clean()` is intended for HTML contexts. This covers both plain text as well as HTML attribute values. I think the previous behavior was incorrect.

However, `linkify` looks wrong. It's probably using the wrong parser. That should get fixed in this PR. I'll look at that either today or Monday.

I'm interested in seeing examples of wysiwig editors that use Bleach in the way you describe. It helps to have a list of actual users so I can run things by people before landing changes.

Thank you again for looking through it!

gen() is called once,  but it returns a generator. The generator is passed to _send, and then requests call next() repeatedly to get the next chunk of data. I profiled a benchmark that inserts millions of rows, and these calls to next() had a significant overhead. Reducing their numbers by batching rows improved the performance of insert(). But if you don't want to use it, you can pass batch_size=1.

I was thinking it might also be nice to have a separate functionality to do experiment launching.

You might have a bunch of files you want to edit and such, and an entrypoint from which your experiment starts. This simple model doesn't always fit nicely in the separation of `setup_commands` or `start_ray_commands`. ie; you might want `setup_commands` to install and start docker, but `start_ray_commands` is going to be executed inside docker. Putting `ray start` in `setup_commands` will cause you do start ray every time you want to execute that block of commands.

 If you don't want ray to restart; you need to put your entry point in `setup_commands` and `start_ray` separately, but this script isn't when you're starting a cluster.

It would make sense to have a separate `experiment.yaml` or something, where you can list all file directories, an entry point, and (optionally) an existing docker container, and `ray execute experiment.yaml` can be run over and over again without restarting or rerunning ray/install commands.

@ivellios, @omab, there are a few issues with using just email address as the main account:

1) if a user already exists with that email address and database has emails as unique, then social-auth will generate an invalid email address for the user.
2) If user does not provide email over social networks (permissions, etc.), it will use FirstNameLastName (username) which is NOT correct if you depend on username being the email address.
3) If user creates social email account and/or regular email account, email sensitivity matters and user is forced to login using UPPERlowerCaSe@email.com (which is normal for email standards but not for users to remember which letters were upper/lower case).
4) Custom case if email is username: If user is already logged in as socialA@facebook.com, and logs in as socialA@gmail.com, then accounts should NOT be linked (validate both emails).

To solve these problems, I created a custom get_username pipeline:

```
from django.http import HttpResponseRedirect
from django.core.urlresolvers import reverse
import urllib

# This is initially from https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-core/blob/master/social_core/pipeline/user.py
def get_username(strategy, details, backend, user=None, *args, **kwargs):
    # Get the logged in user (if any)
    logged_in_user = strategy.storage.user.get_username(user)

    # Custom: check for email being provided
    if not details.get('email'):
        error = "Sorry, but your social network (Facebook or Google) needs to provide us your email address."
        return HttpResponseRedirect(reverse('repairs-social-network-error') + "?error=" + urllib.quote_plus(error))

    # Custom: if user is already logged in, double check his email matches the social network email
    if logged_in_user:
        if logged_in_user.lower() != details.get('email').lower():
            error = "Sorry, but you are already logged in with another account, and the email addresses do not match. Try logging out first, please."
            return HttpResponseRedirect(reverse('repairs-social-network-error') + "?error=" + urllib.quote_plus(error))

    return {
        'username': details.get('email').lower(),
```

If you have a custom User (BaseUserManager) that only takes email address, you need to let social-auth pass the field 'username' or else it will crash:

```
class MyUserManager(BaseUserManager):
    def create_user(self, email, password=None, username=""):
        """
        Creates and saves a User with the given email and password.

        NOTE: Argument 'username' is needed for social-auth. It is not actually used.
        """
        if not email:
            raise ValueError('Users must have an email address.')

        # Validate email is unique in database
        if MyUser.objects.filter(email = self.normalize_email(email).lower()).exists():
            raise ValueError('This email has already been registered.')

        user = self.model(
            email=self.normalize_email(email).lower(),
        )

        user.set_password(password)

        # Save and catch IntegrityError (due to email being unique)
        try:
            user.save(using=self._db)

        except IntegrityError:
            raise ValueError('This email has already been registered.')

        return user

class MyUser(AbstractBaseUser):
    objects = MyUserManager()

    email = models.EmailField(
        verbose_name='email address',
        max_length=255,
        unique=True,
        error_messages={
            'unique':"This email has already been registered.",
        }
    )

    is_active = models.BooleanField(default=True)
    is_admin = models.BooleanField(default=False)

    # Custom: was this email validated, at some point, by sending an email?
    is_email_validated = models.BooleanField(default=False)

    USERNAME_FIELD = 'email'

    def get_full_name(self):
        # The user is identified by their email address
        return self.email

    def get_short_name(self):
        # The user is identified by their email address
        return self.email

    def __str__(self):
        return self.email

    def __unicode__(self):
        return self.email

    def has_perm(self, perm, obj=None):
        "Does the user have a specific permission?"
        # Simplest possible answer: Yes, always
        return True

    def has_module_perms(self, app_label):
        "Does the user have permissions to view the app `app_label`?"
        # Simplest possible answer: Yes, always
        return True

    @property
    def is_staff(self):
        "Is the user a member of staff?"
        # Simplest possible answer: All admins are staff
        return self.is_admin
```

You're trying to connect to the same network multiple times, first by declaring `network` in the `options` dict and then a second time by calling `connect` on the same network.

You can either remove `network` from `options` and let the container connect to `bridge` by default, or you can disconnect then reconnect to the same network:

```python
self.dockcon = self.dockerclient.containers.run(self.dimg, **options)
network = self.dockerclient.networks.get('br0')
network.disconect(self.dockcon)
network.connect(self.dockcon, ipv4_address='172.27.153.11')
```

Ah, I see. If I had to guess, your issue is with the `lastchunk` component of each carray. Each open `carray` reads in the "leftover" portion of data that wasn't big enough to fill a chunk, probably for simplicity elsewhere in the code. Suppose a column with a 32KB chunksize stored 63KB of data, then that `carray` would hold 31KB of uncompressed data. At 100,000 columns, that leftover could quickly become an issue.

Maybe a bcolz developer would have a better solution, but for the moment I think you have the right idea with your workaround. One small suggestion for simplicity would be to reuse the 'names' parameter of the `ctable` constructor instead of making up a totally new function. I threw up an [example here](https://github.com/gogoengie/bcolz/commit/853856a9f383ff461118579ed110099c2ac5e76d) but only had time for a cursory test, so use with caution. Minimally invasive and lets you keep a similar API, by doing `ct = bcolz.ctable(rootdir='files/ctable300', names=['col1', 'col32'])` to open a `ctable` with only a subset of the available columns.

Leaving it to the execution environment tends to not work out well for design-time and tools scenarios. .NET uses 'AssemblyName' IDs to resolve DLL dependencies and resolution rules work a certain way at runtime but are harder for an IDE to follow when there is no specific runtime environment to ask. Tools need to replicate that resolution logic in some way and for that it should be well defined. Starts with a converter that takes an .onnx file and writes the buffers to disk. How is that converter working if it's up to the runtime? A simple approach would be ID is a relative URL to a raw_data byte[] which is stored as just a byte[]. If there is a need to override the ID an environment can do so but loosing the benefits of generic tooling. There are other things to consider like having different sets of weights (model versions) for a single graph.

Subject: [streamlink/streamlink] Include alt streams when parsing HLS playlists (#405)


This fixes #370<https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/370> but also fixes a wider bug in HLS playlist parsing.

When parsing an HLS playlist only one stream was output for each quality, if multiple streams existed at the same quality the ones appearing later in the playlist would be dropped. This patch fixes this behaviour and will output "alt" streams. eg. 360p_alt, 720p_alt, 360p (worst), 720p (best) which allows auto-switching if the primary stream fails (as is in the case with the Toonami stream). ?

The trained neural network seems to have learned a policy which only makes
sense when in contact with ground and with -9.81 as the gravity. Probably
the additional gravity you add pulls the humanoid to ground, which it tries
to compensate with more force to lift up again, so it overshoots since it
is not trained for this and jumps, thus starts flying into the air. And
when above ground, since it was not trained for that either, it performs
pretty random movements, which are too high to be simulated properly. One
could train the humanoid with varying gravity to improve the situation.
I am currently working on making training available for such models. Then
you can improve the network by yourself.

Well, Kinto or Cliquet. The user id should be unique, and the way to do this is to rely to an external authn provider, and all of the services we integrate with are integrable by using OAuth.

I don't know what you mean by "built-in", but I believe we should make it really easy to have the examples integrate with Github / Twitter / Facebook authentication, for instance, and I don't want to have these in the core of Cliquet.

However, having them defined as extra-dependencies can make sense, such as you don't need to do much more than `pip install kinto[github]`.

@dairiki, one example I can think of is the following: I am using per object permissions via ACLs. This means that the final ACL is computed -asking the database- each time the user makes a request. From a utilisation perspective, most performed requests are collection_get (imagine a forum, for example, where users are most commonly watching threads containing lists of posts), where there's no need of computing per item ACLs. So, for such requests, I could have simply added a simple (Allow, Everyone, 'view') ACE that makes no database queries using the collection_acl that I mentioned earlier. But this collection_acl is affecting collection_post as well, which does need to check the database (to check whether the user belongs to a group that allows them to post a new item). 

So, design wise, from a performance perspective, I am not quite happy with not being able to address this issue. On the other hand, the solution you posted earlier (https://github.com/mozilla-services/cornice/issues/322#issuecomment-120001224) does seem to fulfill my needs, but still there may be a different example that will not be able to be addressed that easily with cornice, even though pyramid would allow for an easier approach. 

So, what I imply is that just because I have not thought of an example that could be dealt with multiple routes per request in cornice, doesn't mean that nobody can. I assume that pyramid's architects allowed for multiple routes per request for some reason as well.

Thanks again for all the help.

@bmw I *think* my PR has the same behavior as what you're describing. However my implementation is different.

I only use *one* signal handler function. It always logs signals for later. But if there is a signal while the code block in the context manager is being executed, the signal handler raises `SignalExit` to hand things off to `__exit__`.  Then in `__exit__`, we run the registered cleanup functions, then call/send the logged signals.

Having the signal handler work in both cases avoids the complexity of having to set, reset, and unset handlers for all the signums. And since the signal handler just logs the signals and does not call `call_registered`, it avoids the issues with receiving signals causing `call_registered` to be called twice (as in #3219).

Does this sound reasonable? If you see a discrepancy with the behavior in my implementation, and what you would like, can you write a quick pseudo-code test to illustrate it?

First of all, I'd like to apologize for that terribly ugly dialog window. It's a known issue (#2025).

Let me explain what's going on here. In order to get your certificate, we need to prove to Let's Encrypt that you control that domain and in doing so we temporarily modify your Apache configuration by creating a new `VirtualHost` (see [this](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-02#section-7.3) if you're curious what changes we have to make). To make this vhost and add potential listen directives, we have to know what IP address and port is used for the domain you've requested. We only ask this question if there are no `ServerName` or `ServerAlias` records matching the domain you've requested.

We may be able to improve this behavior, but that's why Certbot asks you the question. When you hit cancel, it falls back to using all interfaces and port 443.

There have been some discussions about this on the forum. In this case, the easiest approach may be to use a different client and use the DNS challenge (which demonstrates your control over the domain by adding a DNS record instead of through a connection to a server).

Certbot is eventually going to support the DNS challenge type, and there is some work in progress on that, but in the meantime I agree that Certbot isn't the best choice for some people with this kind of setup. I think this is best tracked at issue #2236 unless you have a specific proposal for a different way that you'd like it to work.
Though perhaps the most important take-away from this is: why didn't our test suite catch this?
Yes, they are. To bypass currently, you have to use the manual plugin. An option is on the way for reusing a key though, but that won’t be the default.

For what it's worth, I ended up using [acme-dns](https://github.com/joohoi/acme-dns) rather than using the ClouDNS API. It's safer since it's a totally separate DNS server that you can CNAME to (whereas a ClouDNS API key gives read-write access to all your DNS zones)

More specifically, I'm running a server like the @nihilityworld and so far I've traced that the hanging is because:

- it never returns from `nd.SyncCopyToCPU(data, size);` in `MXPredGetOutput` https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/v1.2.0/src/c_api/c_predict_api.cc#L398.
- because it never returned from `this->WaitToRead();` in `NDArray::SyncCopyToCPU`
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/v1.2.0/src/ndarray/ndarray.cc#L1975
- because it never returned from `Engine::Get()->WaitForVar(ptr_->var);` in `inline void WaitToRead()`

@axrdb OK so your issue is the `statement_timeout` setting, which can be set either on your Postgres server (not in Airflow) , in your sessions, and possibly with environment variables on the client (but probably only for psql). There are a few ways to change it.
https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/static/config-setting.html
First is using postgresql.conf file setting in the data directory on Postgres server. If you don't have server access, or don't want to make a global setting that will affect all queries then don't use this.

You can also set it in your session using something like (in milliseconds)
`set statement_timeout to 100000`
You could add that at the beginning of your SQL query being passed in the `sql` parameter for the `PostgresToGoogleCloudStorageOperator` and see if that works. 

There is also an `options` parameter available in the psycopg python package that Airflow uses for connections to Postgres. `options` will pass through any of a range of command line parameters to Postgres. 
The syntax _would_ be something like this:
options='-c statement_timeout=100000'
Unfortunately the PostgresHook doesn't allow for this parameter. The only options are the ones in conn_args, and certain extra ones allowed in the conn.extra field, including keepalives_idle . see:
https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/hooks/postgres_hook.py#L41

In the meantime, you could modify the hook to allow for `statement_timeout` in the conn.extra field. You could do it as a plugin for both the hook & operator, or fork, patch and contribute a pull request. I recommend creating a separate Jira ticket and issue for this, as it doesn't relate to this thread.
Allowing options could be useful for other parameters too, but of course your Postgres user would have to have appropriate permission to set them.

To be clear, keepalives_idle has nothing to do with your statement_timeout issue.
